caption: Hydropic change kidney. The tubular epithelial cells are distended with cytoplasmic vacuoles while the interstitial vasculature is compressed. The nuclei of aff ected tubules are pale.
summary: The image displays a histological section of kidney tissue exhibiting hydropic change, characterized by distended tubular epithelial cells filled with clear cytoplasmic vacuoles and compressed interstitial capillaries. The nuclei of the affected tubules appear pale. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these features, while the right panel is a corresponding H&E stained micrograph at 400x magnification showing the same pathological changes.

caption: Intracellular hyaline as Russell’s bodies in the plasma cells. The cytoplasm shows pink homogeneous globular material due to accumulated immunoglobulins.
summary: The image displays a histological section stained with H&E at 1000x magnification, showing plasma cells with intracellular hyaline inclusions known as Russell's bodies. These bodies appear as pink, homogeneous globular structures within the cytoplasm, resulting from the accumulation of immunoglobulins.

caption: Extracellular hyaline deposit in leiomyoma uterus. The centres of whorls of smooth muscle and connective tissue show pink homogeneous hyaline material (connective tissue hyaline).
summary: The image displays a high-power (400x) H&E stained histological section of a uterine leiomyoma. It highlights the characteristic whorled pattern of smooth muscle cells and connective tissue, with central areas showing pink, homogeneous extracellular hyaline deposits (hyalinization).

caption: Epithelial mucin. Mucinous cystadenoma of the ovary showing intracytoplasmic mucinous material in the epithelial cells lining the cyst.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a mucinous cystadenoma of the ovary, stained with H&E at 400x magnification. It highlights tall columnar mucinous epithelium lining the cystic structures and abundant intracytoplasmic mucinous material within the epithelial cells.

caption: Connective tissue mucin (myxoid change) in neuro fi broma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a neurofibroma stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification. The key pathological feature highlighted is the presence of abundant myxoid material within the connective tissue stroma, which is characteristic of myxoid change often seen in neurofibromas.

caption: Fatty liver. Sectioned slice of the liver shows pale yellow parenchyma with rounded borders.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver section exhibiting features of fatty liver disease. The tissue is characterized by a diffuse, pale yellow discoloration and a rounded, bulging contour, which are classic macroscopic signs of hepatic steatosis.

caption: Lipid metabolism in the pathogenesis of fatty liver. Defects in any of the six numbered steps (corresponding to the description in the text) can produce fatty liver by diff erent etiologic agents.
summary: The image illustrates the pathway of lipid metabolism within a hepatocyte, highlighting six critical steps that can be disrupted to lead to fatty liver disease. Inputs from diet and adipose tissue provide free fatty acids, which are then processed through acetylation, esterification, and glycerol-phosphate synthesis to form triglycerides. These triglycerides are packaged into lipoproteins via lipid acceptor proteins and eventually released as plasma lipoproteins. Defects in any of these steps can result in the accumulation of fat within hepatocytes, contributing to the pathogenesis of fatty liver.

caption: Fatty liver. Many of the hepatocytes are distended with large fat vacuoles pushing the nuclei to the periphery (macrovesicles), while others show multiple small vacuoles in the cytoplasm (microvesicles). Inbox shows red colour in the cytoplasmic fat in the hepatocytes in Oil Red O stain in frozen section.
summary: The image displays histological features of fatty liver disease, characterized by hepatocytes distended with large fat vacuoles (macrovesicles) and others containing multiple small vacuoles (microvesicles). An inset shows Oil Red O staining highlighting cytoplasmic fat in hepatocytes on a frozen section.

caption: Compound naevus showing clusters of benign naevus cells in the dermis as well as in lower epidermis. These cells contain coarse, granular, brown-black melanin pigment.
summary: The image displays a compound naevus, characterized by clusters of benign naevus cells located in both the dermis and the lower epidermis. These cells contain coarse, granular, brown-black melanin pigment, which is visible in both the schematic diagram and the corresponding H&E stained histological section at 200x magnification.

caption: Haemosiderin pigment in the cytoplasm. H & E stain shows golden brown granules in the cytoplasm of macrophages (A) which stain positive in Perl’s stain as Prussian blue granules
summary: The image displays a high-magnification (400x) H&E stained histological section showing golden-brown granular pigment within the cytoplasm of macrophages, identified as haemosiderin. The caption further indicates that this pigment would stain positive with Perl’s stain as Prussian blue granules, confirming its iron-containing nature.

caption: Haemosiderin pigment in the cytoplasm. H & E stain shows golden brown granules in the cytoplasm of macrophages (B).
summary: The image displays a histological section stained with Perl's Prussian blue at 400x magnification, revealing clusters of macrophages containing dark blue granules indicative of hemosiderin pigment deposition within their cytoplasm.

caption: Eff ects of haemosiderosis.
summary: The image presents a classification of haemosiderosis into two main categories: localised and generalised (systemic). Localised haemosiderosis involves deposits in local tissues such as macrophages, fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and alveolar cells, with examples including haemorrhage in tissues, black eye, brown induration lung, and infarction. Generalised haemosiderosis involves parenchymal deposits in organs like the liver, pancreas, kidney, heart, and skin, as well as reticuloendothelial cell deposits in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow. Examples of generalised haemosiderosis include acquired forms (chronic haemolytic disorders, blood transfusion, parenteral iron administration), hereditary forms (idiopathic haemochromatosis due to increased absorption or genetic defects), and excessive dietary intake (e.g., African iron overload).

caption: Brown atrophy of the heart. The lipofuscin pigment granules are seen in the cytoplasm of the myocardial fi bres, especially around the nuclei.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the heart demonstrating brown atrophy, characterized by the accumulation of lipofuscin pigment granules within the cytoplasm of myocardial fibers, particularly concentrated around the nuclei.

caption: Anthracosis lung. There is presence of abundant coarse black carbon pigment in the septal walls and around the bronchiole.
summary: The image displays histological sections of lung tissue affected by anthracosis, characterized by the accumulation of coarse black carbon pigment within the alveolar septal walls and surrounding bronchioles. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key pathological features including a coal macule, dust-laden macrophages, distended respiratory bronchioles, and dilated alveoli. The right panel shows a corresponding high-magnification H&E stained micrograph (X200) confirming the presence of dark pigment deposits in the lung parenchyma.

caption: Nuclear and cytoplasmic changes in necrosis. A, Normal cell. B, Cytoplasm is more pink and nucleus is shrunken (pyknosis). C, Cytoplasm is more pink and the nucleus is fragmented (karyorrhexis). D, The cytoplasm is intensely pink and nuclear material has disappeared (karyolysis).
summary: The image illustrates the sequential nuclear and cytoplasmic changes occurring during cellular necrosis, progressing from a normal cell through pyknosis (nuclear shrinkage), karyorrhexis (nuclear fragmentation), and finally karyolysis (nuclear dissolution), with corresponding cytoplasmic eosinophilia increasing at each stage.

caption: Coagulative necrosis in infarct kidney. The aff ected area on right shows cells with intensely eosinophilic cytoplasm of tubular cells but the outlines of tubules are still maintained. The nuclei show granular debris. The interface between viable and non-viable area shows non-specifi c chronic infl ammation and proliferating vessels.
summary: The image displays a comparison between a schematic diagram and a histological slide of a kidney infarct exhibiting coagulative necrosis. The diagram illustrates the transition from viable renal tissue to an area of necrosis, separated by an inflammatory cell infiltrate. The corresponding H&E stained micrograph at 200x magnification shows the actual pathology: the necrotic area on the right features tubular cells with intensely eosinophilic cytoplasm where cellular outlines are preserved but nuclei have become granular debris, characteristic of coagulative necrosis. The interface between the viable and necrotic zones demonstrates chronic inflammation and proliferating vessels.

caption: Liquefactive necrosis brain. The necrosed area on right side of the fi eld shows a cystic space containing cell debris, while the surrounding zone shows granulation tissue and gliosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue exhibiting liquefactive necrosis. The right side of the field shows a cystic space filled with amorphous cell debris, characteristic of liquefactive necrosis. Surrounding this necrotic area is a zone of granulation tissue, which includes new blood vessels and inflammatory cells. Further peripherally, there is gliosis, indicated by the proliferation of astrocytes with hypertrophic processes. The left panel provides a schematic diagram illustrating these zones, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained tissue section at 200x magnification.

caption: Caseous necrosis lymph node. There is eosinophilic, amorphous, granular material, while the periphery shows granulomatous infl ammation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node exhibiting caseous necrosis. The left panel, stained with H&E at low magnification, shows viable lymphoid tissue adjacent to an area of granulomatous inflammation and central caseous necrosis. The right panel, a higher magnification (H&E, X200) view, reveals the characteristic eosinophilic, amorphous, granular material of caseous necrosis surrounded by a rim of granulomatous inflammation.

caption: Fat necrosis is cloudy appearance of adipocytes, coarse basophilic granular debris while the periphery shows a few mixed infl ammatory cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section stained with H&E at 200x magnification, illustrating fat necrosis. Key features include the cloudy appearance of adipocytes and coarse basophilic granular debris, with mixed inflammatory cells present at the periphery.

caption: Fibrinoid necrosis in autoimmune vasculitis. The vessel wall shows brightly pink amorphous material and nuclear fragments of necrosed neutrophils.
summary: This H&E stained histological image at 600x magnification demonstrates fibrinoid necrosis within a vessel wall, characteristic of autoimmune vasculitis. The image highlights the amorphous, brightly eosinophilic (pink) material replacing the vessel wall, along with nuclear debris from necrotic neutrophils and viable polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs).

caption: Apoptotic bodies in the layer of squamous mucosa (shown by arrows). The dead cell seen in singles, is shrunken, the nucleus has clumped chromatin, while the cytoplasms in intensely eosinophilic. There is no infl ammation, unlike necrosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of squamous mucosa stained with H&E at 600x magnification, highlighting apoptotic bodies indicated by black arrows. These apoptotic cells are characterized by shrunken morphology, clumped nuclear chromatin, and intensely eosinophilic cytoplasm. The caption notes the absence of inflammation, distinguishing this process from necrosis.

caption: Necrosis and apoptosis. A, Cell necrosis is identifi ed by homogeneous, eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclear changes of pyknosis, karyolysis, and karyorrhexis. B, Apoptosis consists of condensation of nuclear chromatin and fragmentation of the cell into membrane-bound apoptotic bodies which are engulfed by macrophages.
summary: The image illustrates the contrasting processes of cell necrosis and apoptosis. Necrosis (A) is characterized by cell swelling, blebbing, rupture, and an inflammatory reaction, with nuclear changes including pyknosis, karyolysis, and karyorrhexis. Apoptosis (B) involves cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, fragmentation into apoptotic bodies, and phagocytosis by macrophages without inflammation.

caption: Molecular mechanism of apoptosis contrasted with sequence of morphologic changes.
summary: The image illustrates the molecular mechanisms and morphological changes associated with apoptosis, a form of programmed cell death. It details the initiators of apoptosis, the process of programmed cell death, and the final stage of phagocytosis, contrasting these with the observable morphological changes in cells.

caption: Dry gangrene of the foot. The gangrenous area is dry, shrunken and dark and is separated from the viable tissue by clear line of separation.
summary: The image displays a medical illustration and a gross pathology specimen of dry gangrene affecting the foot. The illustration highlights the 'dry, shrivelled toes' with a clear demarcation line separating the necrotic tissue from the viable tissue. The corresponding photograph shows a real specimen of a foot with a large, dry, shrunken, and dark gangrenous area on the dorsum and toes, clearly separated from the surrounding healthy, viable tissue.

caption: Dry gangrene of the foot. Microscopy shows coagulative necrosis of the skin, muscle and other soft tissue, and thrombosed vessels.
summary: The image displays a histological section of dry gangrene in the foot, characterized by coagulative necrosis of skin, muscle, and soft tissues, accompanied by thrombosed vessels and bacterial colonies, with an inflammatory cell response.

caption: Wet gangrene of the small bowel. Microscopy shows coagulative necrosis of the aff ected bowel wall and thrombosed vessels while the junction with normal intestine is indistinct and shows an infl ammatory infi ltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of viable and necrotic small bowel tissue affected by wet gangrene. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating the transition from viable intestine to areas of mild chronic inflammation, coagulative necrosis, and bacterial colonies. The right panel is a corresponding H&E stained micrograph (X40 magnification) showing the actual tissue pathology, characterized by coagulative necrosis of the bowel wall, thrombosed vessels, and an indistinct junction with normal tissue featuring an inflammatory infiltrate.

caption: Dystrophic calcifi cation in caseous necrosis in tuberculous lymph node. In H & E, the deposits are basophilic granular while the periphery shows healed granulomas.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a tuberculous lymph node stained with H&E at 100x magnification. It features a fibrous capsule, an area of caseous necrosis characterized by amorphous eosinophilic material, and granular basophilic deposits indicating dystrophic calcification within the necrotic zone. The periphery shows features of healed granulomas.

caption: Dystrophic calcifi cation in degenerated tunica media of muscular artery of uterine myometrium in Mönckeberg’s arterio sclerosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a muscular artery from the uterine myometrium, stained with H&E at 200x magnification. It illustrates Mönckeberg's arteriosclerosis, characterized by dystrophic calcification within the tunica media, while the intima remains uninvolved. The labeled structures include the calcified media, uninvolved intima, lumen, and adventitia.

caption: Metastatic calcifi cation in tubular basement membrane in nephrocalcinosis due to hypercalcaemia.
summary: The image displays a high-magnification (400x) H&E-stained histological section of kidney tissue showing metastatic calcification within the tubular basement membranes, consistent with nephrocalcinosis secondary to hypercalcaemia.

caption: Adaptive disorders of growth.
summary: The image illustrates five adaptive disorders of growth in bronchial epithelial cells: atrophy, hypertrophy, hyperplasia, metaplasia, and dysplasia. Each condition is depicted with corresponding cellular changes, showing how normal bronchial epithelial cells adapt to stress or injury through these mechanisms.

caption: Cardiac hypertrophy. Weight of the heart is increased. The chambers opened up at the apex show concentric thickening of left ventricular wall (white arrow) with obliterated lumen (hypertrophy without dilatation).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a heart exhibiting cardiac hypertrophy. The apex has been opened to reveal the internal chambers, specifically highlighting the left ventricle. A white arrow points to the left ventricular wall, which shows significant concentric thickening. This thickening has resulted in the obliteration of the ventricular lumen, indicating a state of hypertrophy without dilatation.

caption: Nodular hyperplasia of the prostate. There is hyperplasia of both fi bromuscular elements and epithelium seen as areas of intra-acinar papillary infoldings (convolutions) lined by two layers of epithelium with basal polarity of nuclei.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the prostate stained with H&E at 200x magnification, demonstrating nodular hyperplasia characterized by proliferation of both fibromuscular stroma and glandular epithelium. The glands exhibit intra-acinar papillary infoldings lined by a two-layered epithelium with preserved basal cell polarity.

caption: Squamous metaplasia of the uterine cervix. Part of the endocervical mucosa is lined by normal columnar epithelium while foci of metaplastic squamous epithelium are seen at other places.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the uterine cervix stained with H&E at 200x magnification, illustrating squamous metaplasia. It shows areas of normal columnar epithelium alongside foci of metaplastic squamous epithelium, with underlying chronic inflammation.

caption: Columnar metaplasia oesophagus (Barrett’s oeso phagus). Part of the oesophagus which is normally lined by squamous epithelium undergoes metaplastic change to columnar epithelium of intestinal type.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the oesophagus stained with H&E at 100x magnification, illustrating Barrett's oesophagus. It shows the transition from normal squamous mucosa to columnar metaplasia, where the epithelium changes from squamous to columnar intestinal-type epithelium.

caption: Uterine cervical dysplasia, high grade lesion. It shows increased number of layers of squamous epithelium having marked cytologic atypia including mitoses.
summary: The image displays a histological section of high-grade uterine cervical dysplasia, characterized by disordered layers of squamous epithelium with marked cytologic atypia, including koilocytosis and increased mitotic activity.

caption: Telomeres on chromosomes. In ageing, these end compo- nents of chromosome are progressively shortened.
summary: The image illustrates the progressive shortening of telomeres on chromosomes during cell replication, a process associated with aging. It shows three stages: normal telomeres at the ends of chromosomes, shortened telomeres after one round of cell replication, and further progressive shortening after subsequent replications.

caption: HLA system and loci on short arm of chromosome 6.
summary: The image illustrates the arrangement of the Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) system loci on the short arm (p arm) of chromosome 6, showing the crossover between maternal (green) and paternal (light blue) chromosomes and categorizing the loci into Class I, Class II, and Class III regions.

caption: Major pathological lesions and clinical manifestations of HIV/AIDS.
summary: The image illustrates the major pathological lesions and clinical manifestations associated with HIV/AIDS, categorized into secondary opportunistic infections, secondary neoplasms, neurologic disease, and systemic clinical manifestations across multiple organ systems.
entities: Demyelinating lesions of the spinal cord

caption: Organ specifi c and systemic autoimmune diseases.
summary: The image illustrates a classification of autoimmune diseases into organ-specific and organ non-specific (systemic) categories, further subdivided by their immune-mediated mechanisms: antibodies and immune complexes, or T cells. It visually maps these categories to a human figure, highlighting the systemic nature of certain conditions and the localized impact of others.

caption: Typical LE cell. There are two LE cells having rounded masses of amorphous nuclear material (LE body) which has displaced the lobes of neutrophil to the rim of the cell.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of two LE cells, characterized by rounded masses of amorphous nuclear material (LE bodies) that have displaced the lobes of neutrophils to the cell's rim. This is a hallmark finding in the diagnosis of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).

caption: Abdominal fat aspirate smear stained with Congo red.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of an abdominal fat aspirate smear stained with Congo red at 400x magnification. The slide reveals adipose tissue composed of large, clear fat cells (adipocytes). A key pathological finding is the presence of 'circular positivity,' which refers to the apple-green birefringence of amyloid deposits when viewed under polarized light, indicating the presence of amyloidosis within the adipose tissue.

caption: Amyloidosis of kidney. The amyloid deposits are seen mainly in the glomerular capillary tuft. The deposits are also present in peritubular connective tissue producing atrophic tubules and amyloid casts in the tubular lumina, and in the arterial wall producing luminal narrowing.
summary: The image illustrates the histopathological features of amyloidosis in the kidney, showing amyloid deposits primarily in the glomerular capillary tuft, peritubular connective tissue leading to tubular atrophy, and within arterial walls causing luminal narrowing. The left panel is a schematic diagram highlighting these deposits, while the right panel is an H&E stained section (X400 magnification) demonstrating the same pathological findings.

caption: Amyloidosis of kidney. The kidney is small and pale in colour. Sectioned surface shows loss of cortico-medullary distinction (arrow) and pale, waxy translucency.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney affected by amyloidosis. The kidney is notably small and pale, with a sectioned surface that exhibits a loss of cortico-medullary distinction, indicated by an arrow, and a pale, waxy translucency characteristic of amyloid deposition.

caption: Amyloidosis kidney, Congo red stain. A, The amyloid deposits are seen mainly in the glomerular capillary tuft stained red- pink (Congophilia). B, Viewing the same under polarising microscopy, the congophilic areas show apple-green birefringence.
summary: The image displays kidney tissue affected by amyloidosis, stained with Congo red. Panel A shows the glomerular capillary tuft with amyloid deposits appearing red-pink (Congophilia). Panel B, viewed under polarizing microscopy, reveals apple-green birefringence in the congophilic areas, confirming the presence of amyloid deposits.

caption: Gross patterns of amyloidosis of the spleen.
summary: The illustration depicts two gross anatomical patterns of amyloidosis affecting the spleen: (A) Sago spleen, showing numerous small translucent nodules resembling sago grains; and (B) Lardaceous spleen, displaying diffuse translucent gelatinous nodules with a lard-like appearance due to widespread amyloid deposition.

caption: Lardaceous amyloidosis of the spleen. The sectioned surface shows presence of plae waxy translucency in a map-like pattern.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a spleen exhibiting lardaceous amyloidosis. The sectioned surface reveals a pale, waxy, and translucent appearance with a distinct map-like pattern, characteristic of diffuse amyloid deposition within the splenic parenchyma.

caption: Amyloidosis spleen. A, The pink acellular amyloid material is seen in the red pulp causing atrophy of while pulp. B, Congo red staining shows Congophilia as seen by red-pink colour. C, When viewed under polarising microscopy the corresponding area shows apple-green birefringence.
summary: The image illustrates histological features of amyloidosis in the spleen. Panel A (H&E stain) shows pink acellular amyloid material in the red pulp causing atrophy of the white pulp. Panel B (Congo red stain at X200) demonstrates congophilia with red-pink coloration. Panel C (polarizing microscopy at X200) reveals apple-green birefringence, confirming amyloid deposition.

caption: Amyloidosis of the liver. A, The deposition is extensive in the space of Disse causing compression and pressure atrophy of hepatocytes. B, Congo red staining shows congophilia which under polarising microscopy shows apple-green birefringence.
summary: The image illustrates amyloidosis of the liver, showing extensive amyloid deposition in the space of Disse which compresses and causes atrophy of hepatocytes. Congo red staining under polarizing microscopy reveals apple-green birefringence, confirming the presence of amyloid.

caption: Distribution of body fluid compartments.
summary: The image illustrates the distribution of body fluid compartments, showing that intracellular fluid constitutes 33% of total body water, extracellular fluid accounts for 27% (further divided into plasma at 5% and interstitial fluid at 12%), and minerals, fat, and protein make up 40% of the body's composition.

caption: Body fluid compartments compartment; ECF = extracellular fluid compartment).
summary: The image illustrates the distribution of body fluids into distinct compartments, highlighting the percentages of intracellular fluid (ICF) and extracellular fluid (ECF), with ECF further divided into blood plasma, lymph, and mesenchyme. It also details the ionic composition of these compartments, including potassium (K+), magnesium (Mg++), sodium (Na+), chloride (Cl-), bicarbonate (HCO3-), and proteins.

caption: Pulmonary oedema. The alveolar capillaries are congested. The alveolar spaces as well as interstitium contain eosinophilic, granular, homogeneous and pink proteinaceous oedema fluid alongwith some RBCs and inflammatory cells.
summary: The image displays pulmonary oedema, characterized by congested alveolar capillaries and the presence of eosinophilic, granular, homogeneous, and pink proteinaceous oedema fluid within the alveolar spaces and interstitium. Additionally, red blood cells (RBCs) and inflammatory cells are observed in these areas.

caption: Normal haemodynamic flow of blood in the body.
summary: The image illustrates the normal hemodynamic flow of blood through the systemic and pulmonary circulations, highlighting the heart's role in pumping oxygenated blood to the body and deoxygenated blood to the lungs for gas exchange.

caption: CVC lung. The alveolar septa are widened and thickened due to congestion, oedema and mild fibrosis. The alveolar lumina contain heart failure cells (alveolar macrophages containing haemosiderin pigment).
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue from a case of chronic venous congestion (CVC). The alveolar septa are widened and thickened due to congestion, edema, and mild fibrosis. The alveolar lumina contain heart failure cells (alveolar macrophages containing hemosiderin pigment) and intra-alveolar red blood cells (RBCs).

caption: CVC liver. The centrilobular zone shows marked degene ration and necrosis of hepatocytes accompanied by haemor rhage while the peripheral zone shows mild fatty change of liver cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue from a case of Chronic Venous Congestion (CVC). The diagram on the left illustrates the characteristic zonal pathology: the centrilobular zone exhibits central haemorrhagic necrosis of hepatocytes, while the surrounding periportal/peripheral zone shows mild fatty change (steatosis). The photomicrograph on the right (H&E stain, x200) confirms these findings, showing extensive coagulative necrosis and loss of cellular detail in the central areas, contrasting with the preserved architecture and mild steatosis in the periphery.

caption: CVC spleen (Congestive splenomegaly). Sectioned surface shows that the spleen is heavy and enlarged in size. The colour of sectioned surface is grey­tan.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a spleen exhibiting congestive splenomegaly (CVC). The organ is visibly enlarged and heavy, with a sectioned surface that appears grey-tan in color, consistent with the pathological description provided.

caption: CVC spleen. The sinuses are dilated and congested. There is increased fibrosis in the red pulp, capsule and the trabeculae. A Gamna­ Gandy body is also seen.
summary: The image displays histological features of congestive venous congestion (CVC) of the spleen. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating a thickened capsule, congested sinusoids, and a Gamna-Gandy body. The right panel is an H&E stained micrograph at 100x magnification showing the corresponding pathology: dilated and congested sinuses, increased fibrosis in the red pulp, capsule, and trabeculae, and a visible Gamna-Gandy body.

caption: Pathogenesis of circulatory shock.
summary: The image is a flowchart illustrating the pathogenesis of circulatory shock. It categorizes the etiology of shock into Hypovolaemic, Septic (Toxaemic), Cardiogenic, and Other types. These causes lead to a cascade of physiological changes, starting with decreased effective circulating blood volume, which subsequently reduces venous return to the heart, cardiac output, and blood flow. This chain reaction results in a decreased supply of oxygen, leading to anoxia and the release of inflammatory mediators, ultimately culminating in the state of shock.

caption: Response of inflammatory mediators in shock.
summary: The image illustrates the cascade of events in shock, starting with hypoperfusion leading to hypoxic cell injury, which activates innate immunity and stimulates macrophages. This results in the release of inflammatory mediators, including TNF-α, IL-1, and other cytokines (IL-6, IL-12, IL-8, PAF). These mediators trigger various downstream effects: TNF-α leads to nitric oxide synthesis and free radical generation, IL-1 causes vasodilation and hypotension, and other cytokines produce free radicals and complement activation (C3a, C5a).

caption: Major factors in pathophysiology of thrombus formation.
summary: The image illustrates the pathophysiology of thrombosis, depicting a cross-section of a blood vessel with a central thrombus. It highlights five major contributing factors: endothelial injury, the role of platelets, the coagulation system, altered blood flow, and hypercoagulability.

caption: Role of endothelial injury and platelet activation in thrombosis. A, Endothelial injury exposes subendothelial matrix to circulating blood. B, This triggers three platelet steps involving platelet activation: adhesion, release and aggregation. Platelet release is associated with release of granules (alpha granules and dense bodies). C, Concurrent activation of coagulation cascade generates fibrin strands and thrombin forming a tight meshwork called thrombus.
summary: The image illustrates the sequential process of thrombus formation initiated by endothelial injury. Panel A shows the initial exposure of subendothelial collagen due to endothelial damage. Panel B depicts platelet adhesion, release of granules, and aggregation at the site of injury. Panel C demonstrates the subsequent activation of the coagulation cascade, resulting in fibrin strand formation and thrombin generation, which together create a stable thrombus.

caption: Alterations in flow of blood.
summary: The image illustrates two distinct patterns of blood flow within a blood vessel: A) Normal axial flow, where red blood cells are centrally located and white blood cells and platelets are distributed in the peripheral stream; and B) Margination and pavementing, where red blood cells are pushed to the center and white blood cells and platelets adhere to the vessel wall.

caption: Thrombus in an artery. The thrombus is adherent to the arterial wall and is seen occluding most of the lumen. It shows lines of Zahn composed of granular­looking platelets and fibrin meshwork with entangled red cells and leucocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an artery with an adherent thrombus occluding most of the lumen. The thrombus exhibits Lines of Zahn, characterized by alternating layers of platelets/fibrin and red blood cells, indicating a thrombus formed in flowing blood. The diagram labels the arterial wall, residual lumen, and thrombus components, while the H&E stained micrograph at 100x magnification shows the corresponding tissue architecture.

caption: Fate of thrombus.
summary: The image illustrates the four possible fates of a thrombus: resolution, organization, propagation, and thromboembolism. Each fate is depicted with a corresponding diagram showing the structural changes or outcomes associated with that process.

caption: Sources of arterial and venous emboli.
summary: The image is a comparative diagram illustrating the sources of arterial and venous thromboembolism. It categorizes the origins of emboli into two main pathways: Arterial Thromboembolism, which originates from the heart (specifically the left atrium and left ventricle) and systemic arteries (aorta, carotid arteries), and Venous Thromboembolism, which originates from the venous system (deep veins, pelvic veins, cavernous sinus) and travels to the right side of the heart and lungs.

caption: Major consequences of pulmonary embolism.
summary: The image illustrates the major clinical consequences of a pulmonary thromboembolism, depicting the heart and lungs with arrows pointing to six potential outcomes. These outcomes include sudden death, acute cor pulmonale, pulmonary infarction, pulmonary hemorrhage, resolution, and chronic cor pulmonale with pulmonary hypertension and pulmonary arteriosclerosis.

caption: Common locations of systemic infarcts following arterial embolism.
summary: The image illustrates the common locations of systemic infarcts following arterial embolism, highlighting various organs and tissues that can be affected by such events.

caption: Haemorrhagic infarct lung. Infarcted area shows ghost alveoli filled with blood.
summary: This H&E stained histological slide (X100 magnification) of a haemorrhagic infarct in the lung demonstrates key pathological features. The image highlights an area of coagulative necrosis characterized by 'ghost alveoli' (alveolar spaces that have lost their cellular detail), which are filled with red blood cells indicating haemorrhage. Surrounding this necrotic zone are inflammatory cells and areas of oedema. In contrast, the viable lung parenchyma is visible at the periphery, showing normal alveolar structure.

caption: Infarct kidney. The wedge­shaped infarct is slightly depressed on the surface. The apex lies internally and wide base is on the surface. The central area is pale while the margin is haemorrhagic.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting a classic wedge-shaped infarct. The lesion is characterized by a pale central area and a hemorrhagic margin, with the apex of the wedge pointing towards the renal hilum and the wide base located on the kidney's surface.

caption: Haemorrhagic infarct lung. The sectioned surface shows dark tan firm areas (arrow) with base on the pleura.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung exhibiting a haemorrhagic infarct. The sectioned surface reveals dark, tan, firm areas located at the base of the pleura, as indicated by the arrow, which is characteristic of this type of infarction.

caption: Renal infarct. Renal tubules and glomeruli show typical coagulative necrosis i.e. intact outlines of necrosed cells. There is acute inflammatory infiltrate at the periphery of the infarct.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a renal infarct, characterized by coagulative necrosis of renal tubules and glomeruli with preserved cellular outlines. An acute inflammatory cell infiltrate is present at the periphery of the necrotic area, while viable renal tissue is visible in the non-affected regions.

caption: Pale infarct spleen. A wedge­shaped shrunken area of pale colour is seen with base resting under the capsule, while the margin is congested.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a spleen exhibiting a pale infarct. The lesion is characterized as a wedge-shaped, shrunken area of pale coloration, with its base located at the organ's capsule and the margin appearing congested.

caption: Pale infarct spleen. The affected area shows outlines of cells only due to coagulative necrosis while the margin of infracted area shows haemorrhage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a spleen exhibiting a pale infarct. The affected area demonstrates coagulative necrosis, characterized by the preservation of cellular outlines despite cell death. The margin of the infarcted area shows evidence of hemorrhage.

caption: A, ‘Triple response’ elicited by firm stroking of skin of forearm with a pencil. B, Diagrammatic view of microscopic features of triple response of the skin.
summary: The image illustrates the 'triple response' of skin, a physiological reaction to firm stroking. Panel A shows the clinical presentation on a forearm with three components: a red line (erythema), flare (surrounding redness), and wheal (swelling). Panel B provides a microscopic diagram of the dermis and epidermis, detailing the vascular changes (dilated capillaries for red line, arteriole dilation for flare) and edema (wheal), along with nerve involvement causing pain.

caption: Fluid interchange between blood and extracellular fluid (ECF). (HP = hydrostatic pressure, OP = osmotic pressure).
summary: The image illustrates the Starling forces governing fluid exchange between blood and extracellular fluid (ECF) under two conditions: A) normal physiological state and B) acute inflammation. In the normal state, hydrostatic pressure (HP) decreases from arteriolar to venular end while osmotic pressure (OP) remains relatively constant, resulting in balanced fluid movement with no edema. In acute inflammation, vasodilation increases HP at the arteriolar end and decreases OP, leading to net fluid filtration and tissue edema.

caption: The Boyden’s chamber with millipore filter, shown by dotted line. A, Suspension of leucocytes above is separated from test solution below. B, Lower half of chamber shows migration of neutrophils towards chemotactic agent.
summary: The diagram illustrates the principle of the Boyden chamber assay used to measure cellular chemotaxis. The apparatus, a cylinder separated by a porous Millipore filter into an upper and lower compartment, allows cells in a suspension above the filter to migrate downwards towards a chemotactic agent placed in the agar test solution below.

caption: Stages in phagocytosis of a foreign particle. A, Opsonisation of the particle. B, Pseudopod engulfing the opsonised particle. C, Incorporation within the cell (phagocytic vacuole) and degranulation. D, Phagolysosome formation after fusion of lysosome of the cell.
summary: The image illustrates the sequential stages of phagocytosis: (A) opsonization via IgG and C3b receptors; (B) pseudopod engulfment of the opsonized bacterium; (C) internalization into a phagocytic vacuole with degranulation; and (D) formation of a phagolysosome following lysosome fusion.

caption: Mediators of inflammation.
summary: The image is a diagram illustrating the mediators of inflammation, categorized into cell-derived and plasma-protein derived sources. It details the specific mediators produced by each source and their main actions, such as increased permeability, vasodilation, tissue damage, and fever.

caption: Pathway of kinin system.
summary: The image illustrates the pathway of the kinin system, detailing the sequential activation of Factor XII to prekallikrein activator, which then converts plasma prekallikrein into kallikrein. Kallikrein subsequently acts on kininogen to produce bradykinin.

caption: Inter-relationship among clotting, fibrinolytic, kinin and complement systems.
summary: The diagram illustrates the interconnected pathways of the clotting, fibrinolytic, kinin, and complement systems, highlighting how Factor XIIa initiates cascades in all three systems (clotting, fibrinolytic, and kinin), which ultimately converge to produce permeability factors and the membrane attack complex (MAC) via the complement system.

caption: The activation of fibrinolytic system.
summary: The image illustrates the activation pathway of the fibrinolytic system, showing how plasminogen is converted into plasmin by plasminogen activators (such as kallikrein, Factor XIIa, leukocytes, and endothelium). Plasmin then cleaves C3 into C3a and fibrinogen into fibrin split products.

caption: Pathway of the clotting system.
summary: The image displays a schematic diagram illustrating the coagulation cascade, detailing the sequential activation of clotting factors leading to fibrin formation and subsequent fibrinolysis.

caption: Giant cells of various types. A, Foreign body giant cell with uniform nuclei dispersed throughout the cytoplasm. B, Langhans’ giant cells with uniform nuclei arranged peripherally or clustered at the two poles. C, Touton giant cell with circular pattern of nuclei and vacuolated cytoplasm. D, Anaplastic tumour giant cell with nuclei of variable size and shape. E, Reed-Sternberg cell. F, Osteoclastic tumour giant cell.
summary: The image illustrates six types of giant cells categorized into inflammatory and tumor-related groups. Inflammatory giant cells include Foreign body type (A), Langhans' type (B), and Touton type (C), each with distinct nuclear arrangements. Tumor giant cells include Anaplastic tumor giant cell (D), Reed-Sternberg cells (E), and Osteoclastic tumor giant cell (F), characterized by variable nuclear morphology or specific structural features.

caption: An abscess in the skin. It contains pus composed of necrotic tissue, debris, fibrin, RBCs and dead and living neutrophils. Some macrophages are seen at the periphery.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an abscess in the skin, characterized by a collection of inflammatory cells including neutrophils, macrophages, necrotic debris, fibrin, and red blood cells. The diagram on the left illustrates the cellular composition of the abscess, while the H&E stained tissue section on the right shows the corresponding histological features at 100x magnification.

caption: Sequelae of pyaemia.
summary: The image illustrates the sequelae of pyaemia, depicting pyaemic abscesses in the cerebral cortex, myocardium, lungs, and kidney, as well as septic infarcts in the lungs, liver, cerebral cortex, and kidney. The diagrams highlight the distribution and impact of these pathological conditions across various organs.

caption: Fate of acute inflammation.
summary: The image illustrates the potential outcomes of acute inflammation, branching into resolution, regeneration, healing by fibrosis, suppuration, and chronic inflammation, depending on whether the etiological agent is removed or persists.

caption: Morphology of a tubercle. There is central caseation necrosis, surrounded by elongated epithelioid cells having characteristic slipper-shaped nuclei, with interspersed Langhans’ giant cells. Periphery shows lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a tubercle, characterized by a central area of caseation necrosis. This necrotic core is surrounded by a layer of elongated epithelioid cells, which feature characteristic slipper-shaped nuclei. Interspersed within this cellular layer are Langhans' giant cells. The periphery of the lesion is populated by lymphocytes. The tissue is stained using Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 400x magnification.

caption: Tuberculosis of the lymph nodes showing presence of acid-fast bacilli in Ziehl-Neelsen staining.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a lymph node biopsy stained with Ziehl-Neelsen stain, revealing the presence of acid-fast bacilli indicative of tuberculosis infection.

caption: The primary complex is composed of 3 components: Ghon’s focus, draining lymphatics, and hilar lymph nodes.
summary: The image illustrates the primary complex of tuberculosis, which consists of three key components: Ghon's focus (a granuloma in the lung parenchyma), draining lymphatics (lymphatic vessels extending from the focus), and hilar lymph nodes (enlarged lymph nodes at the lung hilum). This triad represents the initial site of infection and immune response in pulmonary tuberculosis.

caption: Sequelae of primary complex. A, Healing by fibrosis and calcification. B, Progressive primary tuberculosis spreading to the other areas of the same lung or opposite lung. C, Miliary spread to lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys and brain. D, Progressive secondary pulmonary tuberculosis from reactivation of dormant primary complex.
summary: The image illustrates the four potential sequelae of a primary tuberculosis complex in the lungs. Panel A depicts healing characterized by fibrosis and calcification. Panel B shows progressive primary tuberculosis spreading within the same lung or to the opposite lung. Panel C illustrates miliary spread, where tubercles disseminate to multiple organs including the lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys, and brain. Panel D demonstrates reactivation of the primary complex leading to progressive secondary pulmonary tuberculosis.

caption: Caseating granulomatous lymphadenitis. A, Cut section of matted mass of lymph nodes shows merging capsules and large areas of caseation necrosis (arrow). B, Caseating epithelioid cell granulomas with a few Langhans’ giant cells in the cortex of lymph node.
summary: The image depicts caseating granulomatous lymphadenitis, characterized by a matted mass of lymph nodes with extensive caseation necrosis and histological features including epithelioid cell granulomas and Langhans' giant cells.

caption: Progressive secondary tuberculosis. A, Endogenous infection from reactivation of dormant primary complex. B, Exogenous infection from fresh dose of tubercle bacilli.
summary: The image illustrates two pathways of tuberculosis infection: A depicts endogenous reactivation where dormant primary complex in the lung apex reactivates, and B shows exogenous infection from a fresh dose of tubercle bacilli entering via the respiratory tract.

caption: Fibrocaseous tuberculosis. A, Non-cavitary (chronic) fibrocaseous tuberculosis (left) and cavitary/open fibrocaseous tuberculosis (right). B, Chronic fibrocaseous tuberculosis lung. Sectioned surface shows a cavity in the apex of the lung (arrow). There is consolidation of lung parenchyma surrounding the cavity.
summary: The image illustrates fibrocaseous tuberculosis, showing both non-cavitary (chronic) and cavitary forms. Panel A depicts a schematic of the lungs with a non-cavitary lesion on the left and a cavitary lesion on the right. Panel B displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung sectioned to reveal a cavity in the apex, surrounded by consolidated lung parenchyma, characteristic of chronic fibrocaseous tuberculosis.

caption: A, Bilateral tuberculous caseous pneumonia. B, Tuberculous caseous pneumonia showing exudative reaction. In AFB staining, these cases have numerous acid-fast bacilli (not shown here).
summary: The image illustrates bilateral tuberculous caseous pneumonia. Panel A depicts the gross pathology of the lungs showing multiple yellowish nodules representing caseous pneumonia. Panel B presents a microscopic view of the exudative reaction, highlighting key cellular components including lymphocytes, neutrophils, epithelioid cells, and macrophages surrounding a central area of caseation necrosis. The caption notes that while numerous acid-fast bacilli are present in AFB staining, they are not shown in this specific image.

caption: Microscopic appearance of lesions of secondary fibrocaseous tuberculosis of the lung showing wall of the cavity.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a lung lesion, specifically the wall of a cavity associated with secondary fibrocaseous tuberculosis. The histological section reveals distinct pathological features including fibrosis, alveoli, lymphocytes, epithelioid cells, caseation necrosis, and Langhans' giant cells.

caption: Microscopic appearance of lesions of secondary fibrocaseous tuberculosis of the lung showing wall of the cavity.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a lung tissue section stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification. It illustrates the histological features of secondary fibrocaseous tuberculosis, specifically highlighting the wall of a cavity formed by the disease process.

caption: Miliary spread by lymphohaematogenous route.
summary: The image illustrates the systemic dissemination of tuberculosis (TB) via the lymphohaematogenous route, highlighting its spread to various organs in both sexes and gender-specific organs. It categorizes TB manifestations into those affecting both sexes (e.g., lungs, spleen, liver) and those specific to females (e.g., mastitis, endometritis, salpingitis) or males (e.g., epididymitis).

caption: Miliary tubercles in lung having minute areas of central caseation necrosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of lung tissue exhibiting miliary tubercles, which are small granulomas characterized by central caseation necrosis. The diagram on the left illustrates key features including epithelioid cells, Langhans' giant cells, and minute areas of caseation necrosis within the tubercles. The corresponding H&E-stained microscopic image on the right (at 40x magnification) shows the actual tissue architecture with similar pathological findings.

caption: Miliary tuberculosis lung. The sectioned surface of the lung parenchyma shows presence of minute millet-seed sized tubercles.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a lung section affected by miliary tuberculosis. The surface of the lung parenchyma is studded with numerous minute, millet-seed-sized tubercles, which are characteristic of this disseminated form of the disease.

caption: The spectrum of lesions in the lungs and pleura in all types of pulmonary tuberculosis.
summary: The image illustrates the various pathological manifestations of pulmonary tuberculosis, depicting a spectrum of lesions affecting the lungs and pleura. It highlights specific conditions including miliary tuberculosis, tuberculous pleurisy, cavitary tuberculosis, Ghon's focus, caseous pneumonia, and tuberculous empyema, showing their distinct appearances and locations within the thoracic cavity.

caption: Lepra bacilli in LL are seen as globi and cigarettes-in-a-pack appearance inside the foam macrophages (Fite-Faraco stain).
summary: The image displays a high-power (X1000 oil immersion) microscopic view of a tissue section stained with the Fite-Faraco stain. It reveals numerous globi and 'cigarettes-in-a-pack' formations of Lepra bacilli located within the cytoplasm of foam macrophages, which is characteristic of the Lepromatous Leprosy (LL) clinical form.

caption: Lepromatous leprosy (LL). There is collection of proliferating foam macrophages (lepra cells) in the dermis, sparse lymphocytes and a clear subepidermal zone.
summary: The image displays histological features of lepromatous leprosy, characterized by a dense collection of foam cells (lepra cells) in the dermis, a clear subepidermal zone, and a thin epidermis with sparse lymphocytes.

caption: Tuberculoid leprosy (TT). Granuloma eroding the basal layer of the epidermis. The granuloma is composed of epithelioid cells with sparse Langhans’ giant cells and many lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tuberculoid leprosy (TT), characterized by a granuloma eroding the basal layer of the epidermis. The granuloma is composed of epithelioid cells with sparse Langhans' giant cells and many lymphocytes.

caption: Organ involvement in various stages of acquired syphilis. A, Primary syphilis: Pri mary lesion is ‘chancre’ on glans penis. B, Secondary syphilis: Muco cutaneous lesions—mucous patches on oral and vagi nal mucosa and generalised skin eruptions. C,Tertiary syphilis: Localised lesion as gumma of liver with scarring (hepar lobatum); diffuse lesions (right) in aorta (aneurysm, narrowing of mouths of coronary ostia and incompetence of aortic valve ring) and nervous system.
summary: The image illustrates the clinical manifestations of syphilis across its three stages: primary, secondary, and tertiary. Primary syphilis is characterized by a chancre on the glans penis. Secondary syphilis presents with mucocutaneous lesions, including mucous patches on oral and vaginal mucosa and generalized skin eruptions. Tertiary syphilis involves localized lesions such as gumma of the liver (hepar lobatum) and diffuse cardiovascular and neurological damage, including aortic aneurysm and neurosyphilis.

caption: Typical microscopic appearance in the case of syphilitic gumma of the liver. Central coagulative necrosis is surrounded by palisades of macrophages and plasma cells marginated peripherally by fibroblasts.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the liver showing a syphilitic gumma, characterized by central coagulative necrosis surrounded by a palisade of macrophages (giant cells) and plasma cells, with fibroblasts at the periphery and a nearby portal triad.

caption: Actinomycosis, sites and routes of infection.
summary: The image illustrates the various sites and routes of infection for Actinomycosis, a chronic bacterial infection. It highlights four primary areas affected: cervicofacial (lumpy jaw), thoracic (lung lesion), abdominal (ileocaecal region), and pelvic (uterine lesion).

caption: Actinomycosis. Microscopic appearance of sulphur granule lying inside an abscess. The margin of the colony shows hyaline filaments highlighted by Masson’s trichrome stain (right photomicrograph).
summary: The image illustrates the histopathological features of Actinomycosis, showcasing a sulphur granule within an abscess. The diagram highlights key cellular components including foreign body giant cells, lymphocytes, macrophages, pus cells, and peripheral clubs surrounding the granule. Microscopic images demonstrate the granule's structure under H&E staining at 200x magnification and Masson’s trichrome staining at 400x, emphasizing hyaline filaments at the colony margin.

caption: Sarcoidosis in lymph node. Characteristically, there are non-caseating epithelioid cell granulomas which have paucity of lympho cytes. A giant cell with inclusions is also seen in the photomicrograph (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lymph node affected by sarcoidosis, characterized by non-caseating epithelioid cell granulomas with a paucity of lymphocytes. The diagram highlights key features such as the capsule, follicle, asteroid body, fibrous tissue, and giant cell. The photomicrograph (H&E stain, X400 magnification) shows a giant cell with inclusions, indicated by an arrow, within the granulomatous tissue.

caption: Common location of lesions in sarcoidosis. The lesions are predominantly seen in lymph nodes and throughout lung parenchyma.
summary: The image illustrates the common locations of lesions in sarcoidosis, highlighting their presence in the meninges, brain, uvea, iris, lacrimal gland, salivary gland, skin, neuropathy, hilar lymph nodes, lung, liver, and spleen. The lesions are predominantly seen in lymph nodes and throughout the lung parenchyma.

caption: Parenchymal cells in relation to cell cycle (G0–Resting phase; G1, G2–Gaps; S–Synthesis phase; M–Mitosis phase). The inner circle shown with green line represents cell cycle for labile cells; circle shown with yellow-orange line represents cell cycle for stable cells; and the circle shown with red line represents cell cycle for permanent cells. Compare them with traffic signals—green stands for ‘go’ applies here to dividing labile cells; yellow- orange signal for ‘ready to go’ applies here to stable cells which can be stimulated to enter cell cycle; and red signal for ‘stop’ here means non-dividing permanent cells.
summary: The image illustrates the relationship between parenchymal cells and the cell cycle, categorizing cells into three types: labile, stable, and permanent. Labile cells (e.g., squamous cells) continuously cycle through G1, S, G2, and M phases. Stable cells (e.g., hepatocytes) typically reside in G0 but can re-enter the cell cycle under stimulation. Permanent cells (e.g., neurons) are non-dividing and destined for cell death. The diagram uses color-coded pathways to represent these categories, with green for labile cells, yellow-orange for stable cells, and red for permanent cells.

caption: Active granulation tissue has inflammatory cell infiltrate, newly formed blood vessels and young fibrous tissue in loose matrix.
summary: The image displays a histological section of active granulation tissue, characterized by an inflammatory cell infiltrate, newly formed blood vessels, and young fibrous tissue within a loose matrix. The left panel provides a labeled diagram highlighting key components such as plasma cells, lymphocytes, macrophages, neutrophils, fibroblasts, neovascularization, and an ulcer. The right panel shows a corresponding H&E-stained microscopic view at 200x magnification, illustrating the cellular and structural features of granulation tissue.

caption: Primary union of skin wounds. A, The incised wound as well as suture track on either side are filled with blood clot and there is inflammatory response from the margins. B, Spurs of epidermal cells migrate along the incised margin on either side as well as around the suture track. Formation of granulation tissue also begins from below. C, Removal of suture at around 7th day results in scar tissue at the sites of incision and suture track.
summary: The image illustrates the three stages of primary union of skin wounds: (A) initial wound with suture track, blood clot, and inflammatory response; (B) migration of epidermal spurs and formation of granulation tissue; (C) removal of sutures leading to scar tissue formation.

caption: Secondary union of skin wounds. A, The open wound is filled with blood clot and there is inflammatory response at the junction of viable tissue. B, Epithelial spurs from the margins of wound meet in the middle to cover the gap and separate the underlying viable tissue from necrotic tissue at the surface forming scab. C, After contraction of the wound, a scar smaller than the original wound is left.
summary: The image illustrates the three stages of secondary union of skin wounds: (A) initial phase with haemorrhage, PMNs, and regeneration; (B) intermediate phase featuring macrophages, scab, plasma cells, neovascularisation, and fibroblasts; and (C) final phase resulting in a collagen scar after wound contraction.

caption: Fracture healing. A, Haematoma formation and local inflammatory response at the fracture site. B, Ingrowth of granulation tissue with formation of soft tissue callus. C, Formation of procallus composed of woven bone and cartilage with its characteristic fusiform appearance and having 3 arbitrary components—external, intermediate and internal callus. D, Formation of osseous callus composed of lamellar bone following clearance of woven bone and cartilage. E, Remodelled bone ends; the external callus cleared away. Intermediate callus converted into lamellar bone and internal callus developing bone marrow cavity.
summary: The image illustrates the five stages of fracture healing: (A) haematoma formation with inflammatory response; (B) granulation tissue ingrowth forming soft callus; (C) procallus composed of woven bone and cartilage in a fusiform shape with external, intermediate, and internal components; (D) osseous callus made of lamellar bone after clearance of woven bone and cartilage; and (E) remodeled bone ends with external callus removed, intermediate callus converted to lamellar bone, and internal callus forming the bone marrow cavity.

caption: Callus formation in fracture healing.
summary: The image displays a histological section of callus formation during fracture healing, stained with H&E at 200x magnification. It highlights key components including osteoblasts, osteoid, newly formed bone, and granulation tissue, illustrating the process of bone repair.

caption: Common stains used for demonstration of microbes. A, Gram’s stain. B, Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) or AFB stain. C, Giemsa stain. D, Periodic acid Schiff (PAS) stain. E, Mucicarmine stain. F, Gomori methenamine silver (GMS) stain.
summary: The image displays six microscopic panels illustrating common histological stains used to identify specific microbes in clinical specimens. Each panel shows a different staining technique (Gram's, ZN, Giemsa, PAS, Mucicarmine, GMS) applied to tissue or fluid samples, highlighting characteristic microbial morphologies such as bacterial colonies, acid-fast bacilli, Helicobacter pylori, fungal hyphae, Cryptococcus, and Mucor.

caption: Forms of plague.
summary: The image illustrates the various forms of plague transmission and clinical presentation. It depicts the transmission cycle starting from a rat flea, leading to a flea-bite on human legs. This can result in a bubonic infection characterized by buboes in the groin and sinus tract. Inhalation of the pathogen can lead to pneumonic plague affecting the lungs. Additionally, the image shows the potential for the infection to become septicaemic (bloodstream infection) or typhoidal (intestinal infection) following the initial flea-bite.

caption: Marked peripheral blood lymphocytosis in whooping cough.
summary: The image displays a peripheral blood smear from a patient with whooping cough, showing marked lymphocytosis characterized by a high density of small, dark-staining lymphocytes. The caption identifies the condition as whooping cough and notes the significant increase in lymphocyte count.

caption: Suppurative diseases caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
summary: The image illustrates the systemic spread of suppurative diseases caused by Staphylococcus aureus, highlighting various infection sites including the brain, respiratory tract, heart, bones, joints, skin, and bloodstream.

caption: Diseases caused by streptococci.
summary: The image illustrates the systemic distribution of diseases caused by streptococci bacteria, highlighting various anatomical sites affected including the brain, throat, lungs, kidneys, and skin, with specific conditions such as meningitis, pharyngitis, pneumonia, lung abscess, septicemia, glomerulonephritis, puerperal sepsis, and impetigo.

caption: Diseases caused by clostridia.
summary: The image illustrates the human body with specific areas highlighted to indicate diseases caused by Clostridium bacteria, including Botulism in the stomach, Clostridial food poisoning in the intestines, Necrotising enterocolitis in the lower intestines, Tetanus affecting the leg muscles, and Gas gangrene in the leg.

caption: Madura foot. Brown granule lying in necrotic tissue in the discharging sinus.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Madura foot, showing a brown granule within necrotic tissue in a discharging sinus, surrounded by an abscess.

caption: Candidiasis of the ulcer in the skin.
summary: The image displays a high-magnification (X1000 oil) histological section of skin tissue affected by candidiasis, an ulcerative fungal infection. The tissue is stained with Periodic Acid-Schiff (PAS) stain, which highlights the fungal elements in magenta/purple. Key features include inflammatory cells infiltrating the tissue, fungal hyphae (branching filamentous structures), and spores (oval yeast-like forms), all indicative of a Candida infection.

caption: Lesions of amoebiasis.
summary: The image illustrates the various lesions and abscesses caused by amoebiasis, a parasitic infection. It depicts the brain abscess, lung abscess, subdiaphragmatic abscess, liver abscess, amoebic ulcers in the colon, periappendiceal abscess, and amoeboma in the colon.

caption: Amoebic colitis. Section from margin of amoebic ulcer shows necrotic debris, acute inflammatory infiltrate and a few trophozoites of Entamoeba histolytica (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of amoebic colitis, characterized by necrotic debris, acute inflammatory infiltrate, and the presence of trophozoites of Entamoeba histolytica, as indicated by arrows. The tissue is stained with H&E at 400x magnification.

caption: Microfilariae in blood film.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a blood film stained with Leishman's stain at 600x magnification. The central focus is a coiled microfilaria, a parasitic worm, surrounded by numerous red blood cells.

caption: Numerous cysticerci in the base of the brain.
summary: The image displays a coronal section of a human brain exhibiting multiple cystic lesions, identified as cysticerci (larval stage of Taenia solium), located primarily in the basal regions. The lesions are characterized by their cystic nature and are indicated by black arrowheads.

caption: Cysticercus in skeletal muscle. The worm is seen in the cyst while the cyst wall shows palisade layer of histiocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skeletal muscle tissue containing a Cysticercus (larval stage of Taenia solium). The parasite's body is visible within a cyst, and the cyst wall exhibits a palisade arrangement of histiocytes. The parasite's cuticle is also labeled.

caption: Lesions produced by TORCH complex infection in foetus in utero.
summary: The image illustrates the various congenital anomalies and lesions resulting from TORCH complex infections in a fetus, including microcephaly, microphthalmia, jaundice, heart disease, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, pneumonitis, and petechiae and purpura.

caption: Gross appearance of haematogenous metastases at common sites.
summary: The image illustrates the gross appearance of haematogenous metastases at common sites, including the liver, lungs, brain, bone, and kidney & adrenal glands. It highlights the pathways of blood flow (portal blood from the large bowel to the liver and caval blood to the lungs) and shows red nodules representing metastatic lesions in each organ.

caption: ABL-BCR gene translocation in chronic myeloid leukaemia.
summary: The image illustrates the chromosomal translocation t(9;22) that results in the Philadelphia chromosome, a hallmark of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). It shows how the ABL gene from chromosome 9 and the BCR gene from chromosome 22 fuse to form the BCR-ABL fusion gene, which is associated with CML.

caption: Sequential stages in chemical carcinogenesis (left) in evolution of cancer (right).
summary: The image illustrates the sequential stages of chemical carcinogenesis, detailing how direct- and indirect-acting carcinogens lead to malignant tumors through initiation, promotion, and progression phases. It also depicts the evolutionary process of cancer from a single target cell to a malignant tumor via initiated (mutated) cells and clonal proliferation.

caption: Consumption of tobacco in India as estimated by weight (Source: National Council of Applied Economic Research, New Delhi).
summary: The pie chart illustrates the distribution of tobacco consumption in India by weight, highlighting that 55% is attributed to bidis, 16% to cigarettes, and 29% to other forms such as chewing tobacco, paan masala, zarda, and gutka.
entities: National Council of Applied Economic Research

caption: Major adverse effects of tobacco smoking. Right side shows smoking-related neoplastic diseases while left side indicates non- neoplastic diseases associated with smoking, numbered serially in order of frequency of occurrence.
summary: The image illustrates the major adverse effects of tobacco smoking on the human body, categorized into neoplastic (cancerous) and non-neoplastic conditions. The right side highlights smoking-related cancers including upper aerodigestive tract cancer, lung cancer, pancreatic cancer, kidney cancer, and urinary bladder cancer. The left side lists non-neoplastic diseases associated with smoking in order of frequency: cerebrovascular accident (CVA), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), congenital heart disease (CHD), peptic ulcer, early menopause, and intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR).

caption: Metabolism of ethanol in the liver. Thickness and intensity of colour of arrow on left side of figure corresponds to extent of metabolic pathway followed (MEOS = microsomal ethanol oxidizing system; ADH = alcohol dehydrogenase; ALDH = aldehyde dehydrogenase; NAD = nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide; NADH = reduced NAD; NADP = nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate; NADPH = reduced NADP).
summary: The image illustrates the metabolic pathways of ethanol in the liver, highlighting two primary routes: the major pathway via alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) in the cytosol and the minor pathway via the microsomal ethanol oxidizing system (MEOS/P-450) in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). Both pathways convert ethanol to acetaldehyde, which is then further metabolized to acetate and ultimately to CO₂ and H₂O. The diagram also indicates the role of NAD⁺ as a cofactor, which is reduced to NADH during these reactions, and notes that acetaldehyde is toxic.

caption: Complications of chronic alcoholism.
summary: The image illustrates the systemic complications of chronic alcoholism, depicting various organ systems affected including the central nervous system, upper aerodigestive tract, heart, liver, pancreas, hematopoietic system, and reproductive system.

caption: Complications of lead poisoning.
summary: The image illustrates the systemic complications of lead poisoning, highlighting its effects on multiple organ systems including the brain, gastrointestinal tract, kidneys, and peripheral nerves, as well as hematological changes.

caption: Major sequelae of obesity.
summary: The image illustrates the major health complications and sequelae associated with obesity, depicting a human figure with labeled anatomical regions affected by various conditions. These include neurological, cardiovascular, respiratory, metabolic, hepatic, biliary, and musculoskeletal disorders.

caption: Two forms of PEM.
summary: The image illustrates two distinct forms of protein-energy malnutrition (PEM): Kwashiorkor and Marasmus. Kwashiorkor is characterized by edema, a distended abdomen, and skin changes, while Marasmus presents with severe wasting, loss of subcutaneous fat, and muscle atrophy.

caption: Normal metabolism of vitamin D.
summary: The image illustrates the metabolic pathway of vitamin D, showing its synthesis in the skin from 7-dehydrocholesterol via UV light, dietary intake of Vitamin D2 and D3, absorption in the small intestine, and subsequent hydroxylation in the liver and kidney to form the active form, 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D (calcitriol).

caption: Lesions resulting from vitamin A deficiency.
summary: The image illustrates the clinical manifestations of vitamin A deficiency, categorized into ocular lesions (including Bitot's spots, night blindness, xerosis, keratomalacia, and blindness), cutaneous lesions (xeroderma), and squamous metaplasia affecting respiratory epithelium, urothelium, and pancreatic duct.

caption: Lesions in rickets.
summary: The image illustrates the skeletal deformities associated with rickets, a condition affecting bone development in children. Key features include craniotabes (softening of the skull), rachitic rosary (beading of the ribs), pigeon-chest deformity (anterior chest wall depression), Harrison's sulcus (a groove on the lower chest), lumbar lordosis (excessive inward curvature of the spine), enlarged epiphysis of the radius (widened ends of the radius bone), and knock-knees (genu valgum).

caption: Lesions in scurvy.
summary: The image illustrates the clinical manifestations of scurvy, a disease caused by vitamin C deficiency, depicted on a human figure with labeled anatomical sites showing characteristic signs such as anaemia, gingival swelling, skin rash, scurbutic rosary, delayed wound healing, ecchymoses, subperiosteal haematoma, haemarthrosis, and bow legs.

caption: Wet (Cardiac) beriberi. Flabby, thin-walled, enlarged and globular appearance of the heart due to four-chamber dilatation.
summary: The image illustrates a diagram of a heart affected by wet (cardiac) beriberi, characterized by a flabby, thin-walled, enlarged, and globular appearance due to four-chamber dilatation.

caption: Nuclear sexing. A, Sex chromatin as seen in scraped squamous cells from oral cavity. B, Barr body seen as drumstick appendage attached to a lobe of a circulating neutrophil.
summary: The image illustrates two methods of nuclear sexing: (A) sex chromatin (Barr body) in scraped squamous cells from the oral cavity, and (B) a Barr body appearing as a drumstick appendage attached to a lobe of a circulating neutrophil.

caption: Classification of chromosomes based on size and location of centromere.
summary: The image illustrates the classification of chromosomes into three types—Metacentric, Submetacentric, and Acrocentric—based on the position of the centromere and the relative lengths of the chromosome arms. It visually demonstrates how the centromere placement affects arm length and overall chromosome structure.

caption: Clinical features of important forms of numerical chromosomal abnormalities.
summary: The image illustrates the clinical features of three important forms of numerical chromosomal abnormalities: Down's syndrome, Klinefelter's syndrome, and Turner's syndrome. Each condition is depicted with specific physical characteristics and associated medical findings.

caption: Common structural abnormalities of human chromosomes.
summary: The image illustrates six common structural abnormalities of human chromosomes, including translocation, deletion, inversion, ring formation, and isochromosome, alongside a normal chromosome for comparison.

caption: Sites of haematopoiesis in the bone marrow in the adult.
summary: The image illustrates the primary sites of haematopoiesis in the adult human skeleton, highlighting specific bones where blood cell production occurs. These include the skull, humerus (proximal end), sternum, ribs, vertebrae, pelvis, sacrum, femur (proximal end), and tibia. The diagram serves to educate on the anatomical locations responsible for hematopoietic activity in mature individuals.

caption: A normal bone marrow in an adult as seen in a section after trephine biopsy. Bony trabeculae support the marrow-containing tissue. Approximately 50% of the soft tissue of the bone consists of haematopoietic tissue and 50% is fatty marrow.
summary: The image displays a normal adult bone marrow section from a trephine biopsy, illustrating the composition of the soft tissue within the bone. It shows that approximately 50% of the soft tissue is haematopoietic tissue (blood-forming cells) and the other 50% is fatty marrow, supported by bony trabeculae.

caption: The erythroid series. There is progressive condensation of the nuclear chromatin which is eventually extruded from the cell at the late erythroblast stage. The cytoplasm contains progressively less RNA and more haemoglobin.
summary: The image illustrates the stages of erythropoiesis, showing the progression from proerythroblast to mature red blood cells. Key features include nuclear chromatin condensation and extrusion, and cytoplasmic changes with decreasing RNA and increasing hemoglobin content.

caption: Reticulocytes in blood as seen in blood stained by supravital dye, new methylene blue.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a blood smear stained with the supravital dye new methylene blue at 1000x magnification using oil immersion. The primary feature is the presence of reticulocytes, which are immature red blood cells characterized by their reticular (net-like) RNA remnants that stain dark blue or black against the lighter background of mature red blood cells.

caption: A, Normal adult haemoglobin molecule (HbA) consisting of a2 b2 globin chains, each with its own haem group in oxy and deoxy state. The haemoglobin tetramer can bind up to four molecules of oxygen in the iron containing sites of the haem molecules. As oxygen is bound, salt bridges are broken, and 2,3-BPG and CO2 are expelled. B, Hb-dissociation curve. On dissociation of oxygen from Hb molecule i.e. on release of oxygen to the tissues, salt bridges are formed again, and 2,3-BPG and CO2, are bound. The shift of the curve to higher oxygen delivery is affected by acidic pH, increased 2,3-BPG and HbA molecule while oxygen delivery is less with high pH, low 2,3-BPG and HbF.
summary: The image illustrates the structural differences between oxyhaemoglobin and deoxyhaemoglobin, highlighting the role of salt bridges and 2,3-BPG in oxygen binding and release. It also presents the oxygen dissociation curve, showing how factors like pH, 2,3-BPG levels, and haemoglobin type (HbA vs. HbF) influence oxygen delivery to tissues.

caption: Normal red cell destruction in the RE system.
summary: The image illustrates the physiological pathway of bilirubin metabolism originating from the destruction of red blood cells. It depicts the breakdown of haem into biliverdin and subsequently bilirubin, followed by its transport in the blood, hepatic conjugation, excretion into the intestine, and final conversion into stercobilinogen and urobilinogen.

caption: Some of the common morphologic abnormalities of red cells (The serial numbers in the illustrations correspond to the order in which they are described in the text).
summary: The image displays a collection of ten distinct morphologic abnormalities of red blood cells, each illustrated with a corresponding label and Roman numeral. These include spherocytes, schistocytes, irregular contracted cells, target cells, sickle cells, crenated red cells, acanthocytes, burr cells, stomatocytes, and ovalocytes, representing various structural deviations from the normal biconcave disc shape of erythrocytes.

caption: Contrasting pathways of absorption and transport of iron, vitamin B12 and folic acid.
