summary: The image illustrates three distinct patterns of intestinal tuberculosis: A) Primary intestinal tuberculosis (tuberculous mesenteritis), characterized by Ghon's focus and enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes; B) Secondary intestinal tuberculosis, featuring a transverse ulcer with thickened wall and stricture; and C) Hyperplastic caecal tuberculosis, showing ulceration, adhesions, thickened caecal wall, and fibrosis of peritoneum.

caption: Intestinal tuberculosis. A, The external surface of small intestine shows stricture and a lymph node in section having caseation necrosis (arrow). B, The lumen shows characteristic transverse ulcers and two strictures (arrow). The wall of intestine in the area of narrowed lumen is thickened. C, Microscopy of intestine shows caseating epithelioid cell granulomas in the intestinal wall.
summary: The image displays pathological features of intestinal tuberculosis, including external stricture and caseating lymph nodes, transverse ulcers in the intestinal lumen, and caseating epithelioid cell granulomas in the intestinal wall under microscopy.

caption: A, Typhoid ulcers in the small intestine appear characteristically oval with their long axis parallel to the long axis of the bowel. B, Blood picture in typhoid fever showing neutropenia and relative lymphocytosis.
summary: The image illustrates pathological features of typhoid fever, including characteristic oval-shaped ulcers in the small intestine with their long axis parallel to the bowel (Panel A), and a blood picture showing neutropenia and relative lymphocytosis (Panel B).

caption: Jejunal biopsy diagrammatic appearance in malabsorption syndrome.
summary: The image displays a comparative diagrammatic representation of jejunal biopsy findings across three panels: A shows normal villous architecture, B illustrates partial villous atrophy with short and broad villi and plasma cell infiltration, and C depicts subtotal villous atrophy characterized by flattened mucosa and plasma cell presence. These panels collectively demonstrate histological changes associated with malabsorption syndrome, highlighting progressive villous atrophy and lymphoplasmacytic infiltration.

caption: A, Partial villus atrophy. There is shortening and blunting of the villi with reduction in their height. There is crypt hyperplasia. B, Subtotal villous atrophy. There is near total fl attening of the villi and crypt hyperplasia.
summary: The image displays two histological sections (A and B) of intestinal tissue stained with H&E, demonstrating varying degrees of villous atrophy and crypt hyperplasia. Panel A shows partial villus atrophy characterized by shortening and blunting of the villi alongside crypt hyperplasia. Panel B illustrates subtotal villous atrophy, where the villi are nearly completely flattened, accompanied by prominent crypt hyperplasia.

caption: Carcinoid tumour. Gross appearance at common locations in longitudinal section.
summary: The image displays the gross appearance of carcinoid tumors at two common anatomical locations: the ileum and the appendix. It illustrates a yellowish tumor mass within the lumen of the ileum (labeled 'ILEAL CARCINOID') and a similar yellowish tumor at the tip of the appendix (labeled 'APPENDICEAL CARCINOID').

caption: Microscopic appearance showing solid masses and trabeculae of uniform, monotonous, small cells with palisading of the peripheral cells.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of a tumor characterized by a nested pattern, uniform monotonous small cells, and peripheral palisading. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these features, while the right panel shows the corresponding microscopic appearance using H&E staining at 200x magnification, revealing solid masses and trabeculae of uniform cells with peripheral palisading.

caption: Lymphoma small intestine. The polypoid growth is seen projecting into lumen while the covering mucosa is ulcerated.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the small intestine affected by lymphoma, characterized by a polypoid growth projecting into the lumen with ulcerated covering mucosa.

caption: Acute appendicitis. Gross appearance of longitudinally opened appendix showing impacted faecolith in the lumen and exudate on the serosa.
summary: The image illustrates the gross pathology of acute appendicitis, depicting a longitudinally opened appendix with an impacted faecolith within the lumen and serosal exudate on the outer surface, along with visible vascular congestion in the appendiceal wall.

caption: Acute appendicitis. Microscopic appearance showing diagnostic neutrophilic infi ltration into the muscularis. Other changes present are necrosis of mucosa and periappendicitis.
summary: The image displays a histological representation of acute appendicitis, featuring a schematic diagram on the left and a corresponding H&E stained microscopic section on the right. The diagram illustrates key pathological features including congested vessels, necrotic mucosa, and neutrophilic infiltration into the muscularis layer. The microscopic image confirms these findings with visible tissue architecture consistent with acute inflammation and necrosis.

caption: Hirschsprung’s disease, diagrammatic representation of the pathologic changes.
summary: The image provides a diagrammatic representation of Hirschsprung's disease, illustrating the pathologic changes in the colon. It shows a transition from an aganglionic segment (lacking ganglion cells) to a ganglionic segment (containing ganglion cells), with the aganglionic segment being distal and narrow, while the ganglionic segment is proximal and dilated.

caption: Diverticulosis coli. The outpouchings on the mesentery communicate with lumen of colon.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the colon exhibiting diverticulosis coli, characterized by multiple outpouchings (diverticula) on the mesentery that communicate with the colonic lumen.

caption: External piles. There are thrombosed veins underneath the squamocolumar anorectal mucosa.
summary: The image is a low-power (40x) H&E stained histological section of the anorectal region, specifically demonstrating external piles (hemorrhoids). The tissue architecture reveals the squamocolumnar junction where the stratified squamous epithelium of the skin transitions to the columnar epithelium of the anal canal. Underneath this mucosa, there is a distinct area of congestion characterized by dilated, engorged blood vessels containing red blood cells, consistent with thrombosed veins.

caption: Fistula-in-ano. The fi stuous tract is lined by non-specifi c infl ammatory granulation tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a fistula-in-ano, characterized by a fistulous tract lined with non-specific inflammatory granulation tissue, as indicated by the H&E staining at 40x magnification.

caption: Hamartomatous polyps: Peutz-Jeghers polyp.
summary: The image displays a histological representation of a Peutz-Jeghers polyp, characterized by a tree-like branching pattern of the muscularis mucosae. The left panel shows a schematic diagram illustrating this branching architecture, while the right panel presents an H&E stained microscopic section at 40x magnification, demonstrating the corresponding histological features.

caption: Juvenile polyp.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a juvenile polyp, characterized by cystically-dilated glands lined by a single layer of columnar epithelium with abundant goblet cells, embedded in a loose, edematous stroma. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating the glandular architecture, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained tissue section at 40x magnification.

caption: Adenomas (neoplastic polyps)—three main varieties.
summary: The image displays three main varieties of adenomas (neoplastic polyps) with their corresponding histological features: A) Tubular adenoma showing branched tubular glands and pedunculated growth; B) Villous adenoma exhibiting finger-like villi, sessile growth, and muscularis mucosa; C) Tubulovillous adenoma demonstrating a tubular pattern with vertical villi. The images illustrate the structural differences between these adenoma types at both schematic and microscopic levels.

caption: Familial polyposis coli. The mucosal surface is straddled with multiple polyoid structures (arrow) of varying sizes many of which are pedunculated.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the colon affected by familial polyposis coli, characterized by the presence of numerous polypoid structures of varying sizes on the mucosal surface, many of which are pedunculated.

caption: Distribution of the primary colorectal cancer.
summary: The image illustrates the distribution of primary colorectal cancer sites within the colon and rectum, highlighting the percentage of cases occurring in each anatomical region.

caption: Colonic adenocarcinoma. A, Moderately diff erentiated. B, Mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma.
summary: The image displays two histological sections of colonic adenocarcinoma stained with H&E at 200x magnification. Panel A shows a moderately differentiated tumor characterized by pleomorphic cells and malignant glands invading the muscularis propria. Panel B illustrates a mucin-secreting variant, featuring malignant glands surrounded by pools of extracellular mucin and also demonstrating invasion into the muscularis propria.

caption: Anorectal carcinoma. The anorectal margin shows an ulcerated mucosa with thickened wall (arrow). The sectioned surface of rectal wall is grey-white and fl eshy due to infi ltration by the tumour.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an anorectal carcinoma, characterized by an ulcerated mucosa and a thickened wall at the anorectal margin (indicated by the arrow). The sectioned surface of the rectal wall appears grey-white and fleshy, indicative of tumor infiltration.

caption: Pathologic staging according to Astler-Coller system. (Also see facing Table 18.12).
summary: The image illustrates the Astler-Coller staging system for colorectal cancer, depicting the progression of tumor invasion through the bowel wall layers (mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, serosa) and lymph node involvement from Stage A to Stage C2, alongside Stage D indicating distant metastases to organs like the liver and lungs.

caption: Anatomy of the liver and its relationship to the gallbladder, pancreas and duodenum.
summary: The image is a medical diagram illustrating the anatomical relationships between the liver, gallbladder, pancreas, and duodenum. It details the biliary tree, including the right and left hepatic ducts, common hepatic duct, cystic duct, and common bile duct, showing how they connect to the gallbladder and the duodenum. The pancreas is also depicted with its head, body, and tail, and the diagram highlights the Duct of Santorini and Duct of Wirsung, which drain pancreatic secretions into the duodenum at the Ampulla of Vater.

caption: Histology of hepatic lobule. The hexagonal or pyramidal structure with central vein and peripheral 4 to 5 portal triads is termed the classical lobule. The fi gure on left shows functional divisions of the lobule into 3 zones shown by circles. The fi gure on right shows hepatic sinusoid perisinusoidal space and cords of hepatocytes.
summary: The image illustrates the histological structure of a hepatic lobule, highlighting its classical hexagonal or pyramidal shape with a central vein and peripheral portal triads. The left diagram details the functional zonation of the lobule into three zones (Zone 1, Zone 2, Zone 3) based on proximity to the central vein and portal triad. The right diagram provides a magnified view of the hepatic sinusoid, showing the arrangement of hepatocytes in cords, the space of Disse, sinusoidal endothelium, Kupffer cells, and Ito cells.

caption: Salient features in morphology of liver in intra- and extrahepatic cholestasis. A, Intrahepatic cholestasis is characterised by elongated bile plugs in the canaliculi of hepatocytes at the periphery of the lobule. B, Extrahepatic cholestasis shows characteristic bile lakes due to rupture of canaliculi in the hepatocytes in the centrilobular area.
summary: The image illustrates the morphological differences between intrahepatic and extrahepatic cholestasis in liver tissue. Intrahepatic cholestasis is characterized by elongated bile plugs within the canaliculi at the periphery of the lobule, while extrahepatic cholestasis is marked by bile lakes formed due to the rupture of canaliculi in the centrilobular area. Both conditions are depicted using H&E staining at 600x magnification.

caption: Complications of hepatic failure.
summary: The image illustrates the various clinical manifestations and complications associated with hepatic failure, including neurological, dermatological, hematological, respiratory, endocrine, and renal complications.

caption: Genomic structure of hepatitis B virus (Dane particle).
summary: The image illustrates the genomic structure of the Hepatitis B virus (HBV), specifically the circular DNA genome of the Dane particle. It depicts the arrangement of various genes (Pre-S1, Pre-S2, S, C, Pre-C, X, P) and the proteins they encode (HBsAg, HBcAg, HBxAg, DNA polymerase) and their relationships within the viral genome.

caption: Consequences of coinfection versus superinfection in combined HDV-HBV infection.
summary: The image illustrates the different outcomes of coinfection and superinfection with Hepatitis D virus (HDV) and Hepatitis B virus (HBV). In coinfection, a healthy person is simultaneously infected with both HDV and HBV, leading to majority recovery with immunity, infrequent fulminant hepatitis with mortality, and rare chronic HBV-HDV hepatitis progressing to cirrhosis. In superinfection, an HBV carrier is subsequently infected with HDV, resulting in majority chronic HBV-HDV hepatitis leading to cirrhosis, less common acute severe hepatitis with recovery, and less common fulminant hepatitis with mortality.

caption: Acute viral hepatitis. The predominant histologic changes are: variable degree of necrosis of hepatocytes, most marked in zone 3 (centrilobular); and mononuclear cellular infi ltrate in the lobule. Mild degree of liver cell necrosis is seen as ballooning degeneration while acidophilic Councilman bodies (inbox) are indicative of more severe liver cell injury.
summary: The image illustrates the histopathological features of acute viral hepatitis, characterized by hepatocyte necrosis (predominantly in zone 3), mononuclear inflammatory infiltrate, Kupffer cell hyperplasia, ballooning degeneration, and the presence of Councilman bodies (acidophilic bodies) indicating severe hepatocellular injury.

caption: Fulminant hepatitis. There is wiping out of liver lobules with only collapsed reticulin framework left out in their place, highlighted by reticulin stain (right photomicrograph). There is no signifi cant infl ammation or fi brosis.
summary: The image illustrates the histopathological features of fulminant hepatitis, characterized by the extensive loss of hepatocytes ('wiped out'), leaving behind a collapsed reticulin framework. The accompanying H&E stain shows the absence of significant inflammation or fibrosis, while the reticulin stain highlights the preserved but collapsed structural network of the liver lobules.

caption: Clinicopathologic course of HBV and HCV infection.
summary: The image is a flowchart illustrating the clinicopathologic course of Acute HBV/HCV infection. It depicts the various outcomes of the infection, ranging from being an Uncommon Healthy carrier to the Most common Subclinical disease, and Common Acute hepatitis. Acute hepatitis can lead to Recovery or Fulminant hepatitis, which can result in Mortality. Persistent infection can lead to Chronic hepatitis, which may result in Recovery, Cirrhosis, or Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

caption: Gross appearance of pyogenic abscesses in the liver.
summary: The image illustrates the gross appearance of a liver affected by multiple pyogenic abscesses, characterized by distinct, round lesions with thick fibrous walls.

caption: Amoebic liver abscess is commonly solitary and its wall is irregular and necrotic.
summary: The image illustrates a diagram of a liver affected by a single amoebic abscess, characterized by an irregular and necrotic wall.

caption: Miliary tuberculosis liver. The hepatic parenchyma shows epithelioid granulomas with small areas of central necrosis and surrounded peripherally by Langhans’ giant cells and lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of the liver affected by miliary tuberculosis. The hepatic parenchyma exhibits multiple small, non-caseating epithelioid granulomas. These granulomas are characterized by a central area of epithelioid cells, surrounded by a rim of lymphocytes and Langhans' giant cells. Additionally, there are areas of fatty change (steatosis) visible within the liver tissue.

caption: Microscopy shows three layers in the wall of hydatid cyst. Inbox in the right photomicrograph shows a scolex with a row of hooklets.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of a hydatid cyst wall, illustrating its three distinct layers (brood capsule, ectocyst, and pericyst) alongside normal liver tissue. An inset provides a magnified view of the scolex, highlighting its characteristic hooklets. The photomicrograph on the right is stained with H&E at 400x magnification, showing the cystic structures within the liver parenchyma.

caption: Metabolism of ethanol in the liver. Thickness and intensity of colour of arrows on left side of fi gure corresponds to extent of metabolic pathway followed (ADH = alcohol dehydrogenase; ALDH or ACDH = hepatic acetaldehyde dehydrogenase; NAD = nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide; NADH = reduced NAD).
summary: The image illustrates the metabolic pathways of ethanol in the liver, highlighting two primary routes: the major pathway via alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) in the cytosol and the minor pathway via P-450 (MEOS) in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). Both pathways convert ethanol to acetaldehyde, which is then further metabolized to acetate by acetaldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) in the mitochondria, ultimately leading to acetyl-CoA and its complete oxidation to CO₂ and H₂O. The diagram also indicates the role of catalase in peroxisomes as an alternative route for ethanol metabolism.

caption: Pathogenesis of alcoholic liver disease.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of alcoholic liver disease, depicting a cyclical process initiated by chronic alcohol ingestion. This leads to direct hepatotoxicity, resulting in fatty change (steatosis). The process is further driven by inflammation (mediated by cytokines like TNF-α, IL1, IL6, TGF-β) and hepatotoxicity caused by acetaldehyde (formation of protein-aldehyde and MAA adducts). These factors contribute to fibrogenesis (scarring) and oxidative stress (free radicals). The diagram also highlights immunological effects such as impaired CMI, Mallory's hyalin, increased liver fat, retention of liver cell water and proteins, hypoxia, and the production of autoantibodies.
entities: Retention of liver cell water and proteins

caption: Fatty liver (alcoholic steatosis). Most of the hepatocytes are distended with large lipid vacuoles with peripherally displaced nuclei.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of fatty liver (alcoholic steatosis). The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating the key pathological features: a central vein, a fat cyst, microvesicles (small lipid droplets within hepatocytes), macrovesicles (large lipid droplets distending hepatocytes and displacing nuclei to the periphery), and a portal triad. The right panel is a corresponding H&E stained micrograph at 20x magnification showing the actual tissue, characterized by hepatocytes filled with large, clear lipid vacuoles and peripherally displaced nuclei, confirming the diagnosis of macrovesicular steatosis.

caption: Alcoholic hepatitis. Liver cells show ballooning degeneration and necrosis with some containing Mallory’s hyalin (Inbox). Fatty change and clusters of neutrophils are also present.
summary: The image displays histological features of alcoholic hepatitis, characterized by hepatocyte ballooning degeneration, necrosis, Mallory's hyalin bodies, fatty change (steatosis), and a neutrophilic infiltrate within the portal tracts.

caption: Alcoholic cirrhosis, microscopic appearance. It shows nearly uniform-sized micronodules, devoid of central veins and having thick fi brous septa dividing them. There is minimal infl ammation and some reactive bile duct proliferation in the septa.
summary: The image displays the microscopic appearance of alcoholic cirrhosis, characterized by the formation of nearly uniform-sized micronodules separated by thick fibrous septa. The tissue lacks central veins within these nodules. Additionally, the septa exhibit minimal inflammation and reactive bile duct proliferation, while the parenchyma shows fatty change (steatosis).

caption: Post-necrotic cirrhosis. Fibrous septa dividing the hepatic parenchyma into nodules are thick and contain prominent mononuclear infl ammatory cell infi ltrate and bile ductular hyperplasia. A few intact hepatic lobules remain.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue exhibiting post-necrotic cirrhosis. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key pathological features, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained tissue section at 100x magnification. The primary characteristics include fibrous septa dividing the hepatic parenchyma into irregular nodules, prominent mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrates within these septa, and bile ductular hyperplasia. A few intact hepatic lobules are noted to remain amidst the extensive fibrosis.

caption: Primary biliary cirrhosis, diagrammatic representation. There are fi brous scars dividing the hepatic parenchyma into the micronodules. The fi brous septa contain prominent lymphoid infi ltrate and proliferated bile ducts. Many of the hepatocytes contain elongated bile plugs.
summary: The image is a diagrammatic representation of Primary Biliary Cirrhosis (PBC). It illustrates the characteristic histological features of the disease, including fibrous scars that divide the hepatic parenchyma into micronodules. The fibrous septa are shown to contain prominent lymphoid aggregates and proliferated bile ducts. Additionally, many hepatocytes are depicted containing elongated bile plugs.

caption: Major clinical consequences of portal hypertension.
summary: The image illustrates the major clinical consequences of portal hypertension, a condition characterized by increased pressure in the portal venous system. Key features include hepatic encephalopathy, esophageal varices, congestive splenomegaly, ascites, and haemorrhoids, all resulting from altered blood flow and collateral circulation.

caption: Cavernous haemangioma of the liver.
summary: The image displays a comparative view of a cavernous haemangioma of the liver, featuring a schematic diagram on the left illustrating key histological features such as cavernous spaces, flattened endothelium, and normal liver tissue, alongside an H&E stained microscopic section on the right showing the actual tissue architecture with dilated vascular channels filled with red blood cells.

caption: Hepatocellular carcinoma. Sectioned surface shows a single, large mass (arrow) with irregular borders and having central areas of necrosis, while rest of the hepatic parenchyma in the upper part of the picture shows many nodules of variable sizes owing to co-existent macronodular cirrhosis.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a liver sectioned to reveal hepatocellular carcinoma. A single, large tumor mass with irregular borders and central necrosis is indicated by an arrow. The surrounding hepatic parenchyma exhibits macronodular cirrhosis, characterized by numerous nodules of variable sizes.

caption: Macroscopic patterns of hepatocellular carcinoma.
summary: The image illustrates three macroscopic patterns of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in the liver: A) Expanding type, characterized by a single large tumor mass; B) Multifocal type, featuring multiple distinct tumor nodules; and C) Infiltrating type, showing diffuse infiltration without discrete nodules.

caption: Hepatocellular carcinoma, typical microscopic pattern. The tumour cells resembling hepatocytes show pleomorphism and are seen forming 2-8 cell wide trabeculae which are separated by endothelium-lined sinusoidal spaces.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of hepatocellular carcinoma, characterized by tumour cells resembling hepatocytes with pleomorphism. These cells form trabeculae that are 2-8 cells wide, separated by endothelium-lined sinusoidal spaces. The left panel illustrates a schematic representation of these features, while the right panel shows an H&E stained histological section at 200x magnification, highlighting the trabecular pattern and cellular details.

caption: Fibrolamellar carcinoma liver.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 100x magnification, illustrating the features of Fibrolamellar carcinoma. Key characteristics include the presence of large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm (oncocytes) arranged in a distinct cord pattern, separated by bands of fibrous stroma.

caption: Metastatic deposits from adenocarcinoma in the liver.
summary: The image displays a histological section of liver tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, revealing metastatic deposits from adenocarcinoma. Key features include an acinar pattern of tumor cells, anaplastic cells, and adjacent uninvolved liver parenchyma.

caption: Metastatic tumour deposits in the liver as seen on sectioned surface. Characteristic features include multiple, variable-sized, nodular masses, often under the capsule, producing umbilication on the surface.
summary: The image displays a diagrammatic representation and a gross pathology specimen of a liver with metastatic tumor deposits. The diagram illustrates key pathological features including multiple nodular masses, umbilication (depression at the center of the nodules), hemorrhage, and areas of necrosis within the liver parenchyma. The corresponding gross specimen shows a sectioned liver surface exhibiting these characteristic findings, particularly the nodular masses and umbilication.

caption: Pure gallstones of various types.
summary: The image displays three distinct types of pure gallstones, each characterized by its unique color and shape: cholesterol stones (yellow-white), pigment stones (mulberry-shaped and dark), and calcium carbonate stones (hard and light-colored).

caption: Mixed and combined gallstones.
summary: The image displays two types of gallstones: mixed gallstones (multifaceted) and combined gallstones (smooth-surfaced). The left panel shows multifaceted mixed gallstones with varied colors and textures, while the right panel illustrates smooth-surfaced combined gallstones with distinct coloration and surface characteristics.

caption: Cholesterolosis of the gallbladder. The lamina propria of the mucosa shows foamy macrophages.
summary: This H&E stained histological slide at 100x magnification demonstrates cholesterolosis of the gallbladder. Key pathological features include a thickened perimuscular layer, the presence of Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses (invaginations of the mucosa into the muscularis), and cholesterolosis characterized by foamy macrophages within the lamina propria of the mucosa.

caption: Major clinical eff ects and complications of gallstones.
summary: The image illustrates the major clinical effects and complications of gallstones, highlighting various anatomical structures and associated pathologies such as cholecystitis, mucocele, gallbladder cancer, choledocholithiasis, biliary fistula, obstructive jaundice, ascending cholangitis, abscess, acute pancreatitis, and gallstone ileus.

caption: Chronic cholecystitis, microscopic appearance. There is penetration of epithelium-lined spaces into the gallbladder wall (Rokitansky- Aschoff sinus) in an area. There is subepithelial and sub serosal fi brosis and hypertrophy of muscularis. Mononuclear infl ammatory cell infi ltrate is present in subepithelial and perimuscular layers.
summary: The image displays the microscopic features of chronic cholecystitis, characterized by Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses (epithelium-lined spaces penetrating the gallbladder wall), subepithelial and subserosal fibrosis, hypertrophy of the muscularis layer, and a mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrate in the subepithelial and perimuscular regions. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these features, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E-stained histological section at 100x magnification.

caption: Carcinoma gallbladder. The lumen of the gallbladder contains irregular, friable papillary growth arising from mucosa (arrow). Two multi-faceted gallstones (mixed) are also present in the lumen.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a gallbladder affected by carcinoma. The lumen contains an irregular, friable papillary growth arising from the mucosa (indicated by an arrow), and two multi-faceted gallstones (mixed type) are also present within the lumen.

caption: Frequency of cancer in the biliary system.
summary: The image illustrates the anatomical structures of the biliary system, including the liver, gallbladder, hepatic ducts, common bile duct, pancreas, and duodenum. It highlights the frequency of cancer in the biliary system, with specific attention to the gallbladder, which is the most common site for biliary tract cancers.

caption: Chronic pancreatitis. There is destruction of acinar tissue and presence of dystrophic calcifi cation. The necrotic tissue is surrounded by mixed infl ammatory infi ltrate with granulation tissue formation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of chronic pancreatitis, characterized by the destruction of acinar tissue and the presence of dystrophic calcification. The necrotic tissue is surrounded by a mixed inflammatory infiltrate with granulation tissue formation, indicating an ongoing healing response.

caption: Complications of chronic pancreatitis.
summary: The image is a medical diagram illustrating the anatomical location and presentation of various complications associated with chronic pancreatitis. It depicts the pancreas with specific pathological features including pancreatic calcification, pancreatic duct calculi, a pancreatic pseudocyst, and ascites. Additionally, the diagram indicates the consequence of these conditions on the small intestine, specifically showing fat malabsorption.

caption: Distribution of carcinoma of the pancreas (numbered serially) and its major eff ects.
summary: The image illustrates the anatomical distribution of pancreatic carcinoma, highlighting its progression from the head to the tail of the pancreas, and its major effects including obstruction of the common bile duct and main pancreatic duct, invasion into the ampulla of Vater and duodenal wall, and metastasis.

caption: Cross-section of the kidney showing gross structures.
summary: The image displays a labeled cross-sectional diagram of a human kidney, illustrating its major anatomical structures including the cortex, medulla, renal pelvis, calyces, and associated vascular and urinary components.

caption: Cross-section of the kidney showing arterial blood supply.
summary: The image displays a cross-sectional diagram of a kidney, illustrating the intricate arterial blood supply network. Key structures include the renal artery and vein, with the renal artery branching into anterior and posterior divisions, which further subdivide into segmental, interlobar, arcuate, and interlobular arteries. The renal vein is also depicted, showing the venous drainage system.

caption: Ultrastructure of glomerular fi ltration barrier.
summary: The image illustrates the ultrastructure of the glomerular filtration barrier, a critical component of the kidney's nephron responsible for filtering blood. It depicts the three main layers of the barrier: the fenestrated endothelium, the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), and the podocyte foot processes separated by the slit diaphragm within Bowman's space.

caption: Structure of a nephron.
summary: The image illustrates the detailed anatomical structure of a nephron, the functional unit of the kidney, highlighting its components including the renal artery and vein, afferent and efferent arterioles, glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle (descending and ascending limbs), distal convoluted tubule, peritubular capillary plexus, vasa recta, and collecting tubule.

caption: Clinical manifestations in chronic kidney disease.
summary: The image illustrates the clinical manifestations of chronic kidney disease, highlighting various systemic complications affecting different organ systems. These include neurological issues like encephalopathy and peripheral neuropathy, cardiovascular problems such as anaemia, platelet dysfunction, pleurisy, pulmonary oedema, CHF, hypertension, and pericarditis, gastrointestinal issues like peptic ulcer, renal complications such as renal osteodystrophy, osteomalacia, and osteitis fibrosa, skin manifestations like uraemic frost, and other conditions like infections and gout.

caption: Cystic diseases of kidney.
summary: The image illustrates four distinct types of cystic kidney diseases, each depicted with a corresponding anatomical diagram showing the structural abnormalities characteristic of the condition.

caption: Renal cystic dysplasia. There are cysts lined by fl attened epithelium while the intervening parenchyma consists of primitive connective tissue and cartilage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of renal cystic dysplasia, characterized by dilated cysts lined by flattened epithelium and intervening parenchyma composed of primitive mesenchyme and cartilage.

caption: Polycystic kidney disease. Diagrammatic representation of comparison of gross appearance of the two main forms.
summary: The image provides a diagrammatic comparison of the gross appearance of the two main forms of polycystic kidney disease: adult type (ADPKD) and infantile type (ARPKD). It illustrates the external and sectioned surfaces of each, highlighting key pathological features such as lobulation, variable-sized cysts, tiny fusiform cysts, and an enlarged kidney.

caption: Adult (autosomal dominant) polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). The kidney is enlarged and heavy. Sectioned surface shows loss of demarcation between cortex and medulla and replacement of the entire renal parenchyma by cysts varying in diameter from a few millimeters to 4-5 cm. These cysts are not communicating with the pelvi- calyceal system. The renal pelvis and calyces are distorted due to cystic change.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney affected by Adult (autosomal dominant) polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). The kidney is notably enlarged and heavy. The sectioned surface reveals a loss of demarcation between the cortex and medulla, with the entire renal parenchyma replaced by numerous cysts of varying sizes (millimeters to 4-5 cm). These cysts do not communicate with the pelvi-calyceal system, which is distorted due to the cystic changes.

caption: Flea-bitten kidney. The kidney is enlarged in size and weight. The cortex shows tiny petechial haemorrhages visible through the capsule (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a 'flea-bitten kidney,' characterized by diffuse, tiny petechial hemorrhages on the renal cortex visible through the capsule. The kidney is noted to be enlarged in size and weight.

caption: Acute post-streptococcal GN, light microscopic appearance. There is increased cellularity due to proliferation of mesangial cells, endothelial cells and some epithelial cells and infi ltration of the tuft by neutrophils and monocytes.
summary: The image displays a light microscopic view of a kidney glomerulus affected by acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (GN). The diagram on the left illustrates key pathological features including proliferated mesangial cells, endothelial cells, and acute inflammatory cells within a hypercellular glomerulus. The corresponding H&E stained histological section on the right (X400 magnification) shows dense cellular infiltration consistent with the described proliferation and inflammation.

caption: Acute glomerulonephritis, diagrammatic represen tation of ultrastructure of a portion of glomerular lobule showing characteristic electron-dense irregular deposits or ‘humps’ on the epithelial side of the GBM.
summary: The image is a diagrammatic representation of the ultrastructure of a glomerular lobule in acute glomerulonephritis, highlighting key pathological features including subepithelial electron-dense deposits (described as 'lumpy-bumpy'), proliferated endothelial cells, polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs), and mesangial cell hyperplasia.

caption: RPGN (post-infectious type), light microscopic appearance. There are crescents in Bowman’s space forming adhesions between the glomerular tuft and Bowman’s capsule. The tuft shows hypercellularity and leucocytic infi ltration.
summary: The image displays a light microscopic view of a kidney glomerulus affected by Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN), specifically the post-infectious type. Key pathological features include the formation of crescents in Bowman's space, adhesions between the glomerular tuft and Bowman's capsule, hypercellularity of the glomerulus, and neutrophilic infiltration within the tuft.

caption: RPGN, diagrammatic representation of ultrastructure of a portion of glomerular lobule showing epithelial crescent formation and subepithelial granular deposits.
summary: The image is a diagrammatic representation of the ultrastructure of a glomerular lobule, illustrating the pathological features of Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN). It depicts the formation of an epithelial crescent, characterized by the proliferation of endothelial cells and the presence of fibrin. Additionally, it shows subepithelial granular deposits, focally flattened podocytes, focally disrupted glomerular basement membrane (GBM), and mesangial cell hyperplasia.

caption: Minimal change disease. A, Light microscopy shows a normal glomerulus while tubules show cytoplasmic vacuolation and proteinaceous material. B, Diagrammatic representation of ultrastructure of a portion of glomerular lobule showing diff use fusion or fl attening of foot processes of visceral epithelial cells (podocytes). The GBM is normal and there are no deposits.
summary: The image pair illustrates Minimal Change Disease (MCD) of the kidney. Panel A shows a light microscopy (H&E stain, 400x) of a glomerulus that appears structurally normal, with surrounding tubules exhibiting cytoplasmic vacuolation and proteinaceous material. Panel B provides a diagrammatic representation of the glomerular ultrastructure, highlighting the diffuse flattening or fusion of podocyte foot processes and confirming the absence of deposits on the glomerular basement membrane (GBM).

caption: Membranous GN, light microscopic appearance. Glomeruli are normocellular but the capillary walls are diff usely thickened due to duplication of the GBM.
summary: The image displays a comparison of normal versus pathological glomerular structure in the context of Membranous Glomerulonephritis (GN). The left panel illustrates a schematic diagram showing normal glomerular cellularity alongside diffusely thickened glomerular basement membrane (GBM) with duplication. The right panel presents an H&E stained histological section at 400x magnification, demonstrating the same pathological features: normocellular glomeruli with capillary walls that are diffusely thickened due to GBM duplication, characteristic of Membranous GN.

caption: Membranous GN, diagrammatic representation of ultrastructure of a portion of glomerular lobule showing subepithelial deposits of electron-dense material so that the basement membrane material protrudes between these deposits.
summary: The image is a diagrammatic representation of the ultrastructure of a glomerular lobule in Membranous Glomerulonephritis (Membranous GN). It illustrates subepithelial deposits of electron-dense material, which appear as 'spikes' protruding from the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) between these deposits. Additionally, the GBM shows thickening due to duplication.

caption: Membranoproliferative GN, light microscopic appearance. The glomerular tufts show lobulation and mesangial hypercellularity. There is increase in the mesangial matrix between the capillaries. There is widespread thickening of the GBM.
summary: The image displays a comparison between a schematic diagram and a high-magnification H&E-stained histological section of a kidney glomerulus affected by Membranoproliferative Glomerulonephritis (MPGN). Both visuals highlight the characteristic pathological features of this condition, including the lobulation of the glomerular tuft, hypercellularity within the mesangium, increased mesangial matrix, and the thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) with a 'double contour' or 'splitting' appearance.

caption: MPGN, diagrammatic representation of ultrastructure of a portion of glomerular lobule showing features of type I (left half ) and type II (right half ) MPGN. Type I (classic form) shows the characteristic subendothelial electron-dense deposits, while type II (dense deposit disease) is characterised by intramembranous dense deposits.
summary: The image is a diagrammatic representation of the ultrastructure of a portion of a glomerular lobule, illustrating the features of Type I and Type II Membranoproliferative Glomerulonephritis (MPGN). Type I MPGN is characterized by subendothelial electron-dense deposits and proliferated endothelial cells, while Type II MPGN, also known as dense deposit disease, is characterized by intramembranous dense deposits. The diagram also highlights mesangial cell hyperplasia.

caption: A, Focal GN. The characteristic feature is the cellular proliferation in some glomeruli and in one or two lobules of the aff ected glomeruli i.e. focal and segmental proliferative change. B, C, Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. The features are focal and segmental involvement of the glomeruli by sclerosis and hyalinosis and mesangial hypercellularity.
summary: The image displays histological sections of kidney tissue illustrating two distinct pathological conditions: Focal Glomerulonephritis (GN) and Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Panel A shows focal glomerulonephritis characterized by cellular proliferation in some glomeruli and lobules. Panels B and C depict FSGS, featuring focal and segmental involvement of glomeruli with sclerosis, hyalinosis, and mesangial hypercellularity.

caption: End-stage kidney, gross appearance of short contract kidney in chronic glomerulonephritis. The kidney is small and contracted. The capsule is adherent to the cortex and has diff usely granular cortical surface.
summary: The image displays a pathological specimen of an end-stage kidney affected by chronic glomerulonephritis. The left panel is a diagram illustrating the characteristic 'granular surface' of the contracted kidney, while the right panel shows the actual gross appearance of the small, shrunken, and diffusely granular kidney with an adherent capsule.

caption: End-stage kidney in chronic GN, light microscopy. Glomerular tufts are acellular and completely hyalinised. Blood vessels in the interstitium are hyalinised and thickened while the interstitium shows fi ne fi brosis and a few chronic infl ammatory cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an end-stage kidney affected by chronic glomerulonephritis (GN). The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key pathological features: thickened blood vessels, atrophied tubules, chronic inflammatory cells, interstitial fibrosis, and acellular, hyalinized glomeruli. The right panel shows the corresponding H&E-stained light microscopy image at 200x magnification, confirming these findings with visible hyalinized glomerular tufts, fibrotic interstitium, and scattered chronic inflammatory cells.

caption: Diabetic glomerulosclerosis. A, Diff use lesions. The characteristic features are diff use involvement of the glomeruli showing thickening of the GBM and diff use increase in the mesangial matrix with mild proliferation of mesangial cells and exudative lesions (fi brin caps and capsular drops). B, Nodular lesion (Kimmelstiel-Wilson Lesion). There are one or more hyaline nodules within the lobules of glomeruli, surrounded peripherally by glomerular capillaries with thickened walls.
summary: The image illustrates two types of diabetic glomerulosclerosis: A) Diffuse glomerulosclerosis, characterized by diffuse thickening of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), increased mesangial matrix, and exudative lesions such as fibrin caps and capsular drops; and B) Nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesion), featuring discrete hyaline nodules within glomerular lobules, surrounded by capillaries with thickened walls.

caption: Diabetic nephropathy—nodular (Kimmelstiel-Wilson or KW) lesions.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a kidney glomerulus stained with H&E at 200x magnification, demonstrating classic features of diabetic nephropathy. Key pathological findings include nodular deposits (Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions), compressed capillaries, and increased mesangial matrix, which are characteristic of advanced diabetic kidney disease.

caption: Pathogenesis of ATN.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN), detailing the mechanisms by which both ischaemic and toxic causes lead to tubular damage, reduced glomerular filtration rate (GFR), and oliguria. It highlights the progression from initial insults like shock, crush injuries, or exposure to toxins to downstream effects such as tubular obstruction, interstitial oedema, and ultimately renal failure.
entities: Leakage of tubular fluid into interstitium

caption: Ischaemic ATN. There is focal necrosis along the nephron involving proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) as well as distal convoluted tubule (DCT). The aff ected tubules are dilated, their lumina contain casts (hyaline or pigmented haem) and the aff ected regions are lined by regenerating thin and fl at epithelium.
summary: The image displays histological features of Ischaemic Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN), characterized by focal necrosis affecting both proximal convoluted tubules (PCT) and distal convoluted tubules (DCT). Affected tubules are dilated with luminal casts (hyaline or pigmented haem), and the epithelium is lined by regenerating thin and flat cells. Glomeruli remain uninvolved.

caption: Toxic ATN. There is extensive necrosis of epithelial cells involving predominantly proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) diff usely. The necrosed cells are desquamated into the tubular lumina and may undergo dystrophic calcifi cation. The tubular lumina contain casts (granular) and the regenerating fl at epithelium lines the necrosed tubule.
summary: The image displays a histological slide of Toxic Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN). The left panel provides a schematic diagram illustrating the key pathological features: extensive necrosis of the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) epithelial cells, which desquamate into the tubular lumen. These lumina contain granular casts and areas of dystrophic calcification. The diagram also highlights that the glomeruli remain uninvolved and the tubular basement membrane is intact. The right panel shows the corresponding high-power H&E stained tissue section (X200 magnification), visualizing the necrotic tubules and cellular debris.

caption: Pathogenesis of refl ux nephropathy.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of reflux nephropathy, depicting two primary routes of infection: haematogenous infection entering via the renal artery and ascending infection originating from the urinary bladder. It highlights anatomical and pathological features including dilated calyces, intrarenal reflux, vesicoureteric reflux, a short intravesical ureter, and urinary bladder infection, all contributing to the development of reflux nephropathy.

caption: Pyonephrosis. The kidney is enlarged and has increased perinephric fat in the hilum. Sectioned surface shows markedly dilated pelvis and calyces having irregular and ragged inner surface and containing necrotic debris and pus.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting pyonephrosis. The kidney is significantly enlarged with increased perinephric fat in the hilum. The sectioned surface reveals a markedly dilated renal pelvis and calyces with irregular, ragged inner surfaces, containing necrotic debris and pus.

caption: The two major types of mechanisms in chronic pyelonephritis. A, Vesicoureteric refl ux causing infection of peripheral papillae and consequent scars at the poles of the kidney. B, Obstructive pyelonephritis due to obstruction of the urinary tract causing high pressure backfl ow of urine and infection of all the papillae and consequent diff use scarring of the kidney and thinning of the cortex.
summary: The image illustrates the two primary mechanisms leading to chronic pyelonephritis: vesicoureteral reflux and obstructive uropathy. In vesicoureteral reflux, urine flows backward from the bladder to the kidney, causing infection at the poles and resulting in irregular scarring and a thickened capsule. In obstructive uropathy, urinary tract obstruction leads to high-pressure backflow, infecting all papillae and causing diffuse scarring and cortical thinning.

caption: Small contracted kidney in chronic pyelonephritis with calyectasis. A, Diagrammatic representation. B, External surface of small and contracted kidney. The kidney is small in size and contracted. The capsule is adherent to the cortex and has irregular scars on the surface. C, Sectioned surface shows dilated pelvi-calyceal system with atrophied and thin peripheral cortex and increased hilar fat extending inside.
summary: The image illustrates a small contracted kidney affected by chronic pyelonephritis, featuring calyectasis. Panel A provides a diagrammatic representation highlighting key features such as a small and asymmetrically contracted kidney, depressed (U-shaped) scars, blunted calyces, and a dilated pelvis. Panels B and C show the external and sectioned surfaces of the kidney, respectively, demonstrating the adherent capsule with irregular scars, atrophied and thin peripheral cortex, and increased hilar fat extending into the kidney.

caption: Chronic pyelonephritis. The scarred area shows atrophy of some tubules and dilatation of others which contain colloid casts (thyroidisation). The tubules are surrounded by abundant fi brous tissue and chronic interstitial infl ammatory reaction. The blood vessels included are thick-walled and the glomeruli show periglomerular fi brosis.
summary: The image displays histological features of chronic pyelonephritis, characterized by tubular atrophy, dilatation with colloid casts (thyroidisation), interstitial fibrosis, chronic inflammatory cell infiltration, periglomerular fibrosis, and hyalinised vessels and glomeruli.

caption: Causes of obstructive uropathy.
summary: The image illustrates the various anatomical and pathological causes of obstructive uropathy, a condition where urine flow is blocked within the urinary tract. It depicts obstructions at multiple levels: the kidney (stenosis, stone, pelvic/ureteric tumors), the ureter (blood clot, inflammatory stenosis, congenital stenosis, adhesion, loop, external compression, ureteric stone), the bladder (bladder cancer), the bladder neck (contraction), the prostate (prostatic carcinoma, prostatic hyperplasia), the urethra (posterior urethral valve, stricture, meatal stenosis), and the external genitalia (phimosis).

caption: Staghorn renal stone with chronic pyelonephritis. The kidney is enlarged and heavy. The capsule is adherent to the cortex and has irregular scars on the surface. Sectioned surface shows dilated pelvi- calyceal system with atrophied and thin peripheral cortex. The pelvis of the kidney contains a single, large, soft yellow white stone taking the contour of the pelvi-calyceal system (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting chronic pyelonephritis and a staghorn renal stone. The kidney is enlarged and heavy with an adherent capsule and irregular surface scars. The sectioned surface reveals a dilated pelvicalyceal system and an atrophied, thin peripheral cortex. A large, soft, yellow-white stone occupies the pelvicalyceal system, conforming to its shape, as indicated by the arrow.

caption: Hydronephrosis, stages in its evolution.
summary: The image illustrates the two main stages of hydronephrosis: extrarenal and intrarenal. In extrarenal hydronephrosis, the renal pelvis is dilated while the renal cortex remains relatively intact. In intrarenal hydronephrosis, the dilation extends into the renal parenchyma, causing thinning of the renal cortex and external lobulations, indicating more advanced disease progression.

caption: Hydronephrosis with nephrolithiasis. The kidney is enlarged and heavy. On cut section, the renal pelvis and calyces are dilated and cystic and contain a large stone in the pelvis of the kidney (arrow). The cystic change is seen to extend into renal parenchyma, compressing the cortex as a thin rim at the periphery. Unlike polycystic kidney, however, these cysts are communicating with the pelvi-calyceal system.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a kidney exhibiting hydronephrosis and nephrolithiasis. The kidney is enlarged and heavy, with the renal pelvis and calyces dilated and cystic, containing a large stone in the pelvis. The cystic change extends into the renal parenchyma, compressing the cortex as a thin rim at the periphery. Unlike polycystic kidney disease, these cysts communicate with the pelvi-calyceal system.

caption: The renin-angiotensin mechanism.
summary: The image illustrates the renin-angiotensin mechanism, a physiological pathway regulating blood pressure and fluid balance. It begins with decreased cardiac output leading to renal ischaemia, which reduces glomerular filtration rate (GFR). This triggers renin synthesis from juxtaglomerular (JG) cells in the kidney. Renin circulates and converts angiotensinogen (produced in the liver) into angiotensin I, which is then converted to angiotensin II in the lungs by convertase. Angiotensin II acts on AT₁ and AT₂ receptors, stimulating aldosterone release from the adrenal cortex, which promotes sodium and water retention to restore blood pressure.

caption: Renal cell carcinoma. The tumour shows solid masses and acini of uniform-appearing tumour cells. Clear cells predominate in the tumour while the stroma is composed of fi ne and delicate fi brous tissue.
summary: The image displays a comparison between a schematic diagram and a histological slide of Renal Cell Carcinoma. The diagram illustrates the tumor's architecture, highlighting uniform tumor cells arranged in solid masses and acini (glandular pattern), with a predominance of clear cells and some granular cells. It also indicates the presence of residual compressed renal parenchyma. The corresponding H&E stained slide at 200x magnification shows the actual tissue, characterized by sheets of clear cells with delicate fibrous stroma and a visible glomerulus.

caption: Nephroblastoma (Wilms’ tumour). The kidney is enlarged and has ovoid and nodular appearance. The sectioned surface shows replacement of almost whole kidney by the tumour leaving a thin strip of compressed renal tissue at lower end (arrow). Cut section of the tumour is gray white, fl eshy and has small areas of haemorrhages and necrosis.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen and a corresponding diagram of a nephroblastoma (Wilms' tumour). The kidney is shown to be enlarged with an ovoid and nodular appearance, where the tumor mass replaces almost the entire organ. A thin strip of compressed renal parenchyma is visible at the lower end. The cut surface of the tumor is described as gray-white and fleshy, with small areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

caption: Wilms’ tumour. There is predominance of small round to spindled sarcomatoid tumour cells. A few abortive tubules and poorly- formed glomerular structures are present in it.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Wilms' tumour, characterized by a predominance of small round to spindled sarcomatoid tumour cells. The diagram on the left highlights key features including abortive tubules, tumour cells, and poorly-formed glomerular structures, while the H&E stained micrograph on the right (X200 magnification) shows the corresponding tissue architecture.

caption: Gross patterns of epithelial bladder tumours.
summary: The image illustrates the gross patterns of epithelial bladder tumours, categorized into papillary and non-papillary types. Papillary tumours (90%) are depicted as non-invasive papilloma and invasive papillary carcinoma, showing growth patterns from the bladder wall. Non-papillary tumours (10%) are shown as flat non-invasive carcinoma and flat invasive carcinoma, indicating surface-level growth without papillary structures.

caption: Carcinoma urinary bladder. The mucosal surface shows papillary tumour fl oating in the lumen (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a urinary bladder that has been opened to reveal its internal mucosal surface. A distinct, exophytic, papillary tumor mass is visible floating within the bladder lumen, indicated by an arrow. The surrounding bladder wall and mucosa appear relatively intact, contrasting with the abnormal growth.

caption: Urothelial (Transitional cell) carcinoma, low grade. There is increase in the number of layers of epithelium in an orderly manner and slight loss of polarity. The cells show slight nuclear enlargement and mild variation in nuclear size and shape and infrequent mitosis.
summary: The image displays a low-grade urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma, characterized by an orderly increase in the number of epithelial layers with slight loss of polarity. The cells exhibit mild nuclear enlargement and variation in nuclear size and shape, with infrequent mitosis. The left panel illustrates a schematic representation of the papillomatous tumor with stromal invasion and anaplastic tumor cells, while the right panel shows a histological section stained with H&E at 200x magnification.

caption: Tuberculous epididymo-orchitis. The interstitium contains several epithelioid cell granulomas with central areas of caseation necrosis. These granulomas are surrounded by Langhans’ giant cells and mantle of lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tuberculous epididymo-orchitis, characterized by the presence of epithelioid cell granulomas with central caseation necrosis. These granulomas are surrounded by Langhans' giant cells and a mantle of lymphocytes, with areas of fibrosis and uninvolved parenchyma also visible.

caption: Haematocele testis. Sectioned surface of the sac shows thick wall coated internally by brownish, tan and necrotic material which is organised blood clot (arrow).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a testis with a haematocele. The sectioned surface of the tunica vaginalis sac reveals a thickened wall coated internally by brownish, tan, and necrotic material, identified as an organized blood clot (indicated by the arrow).

caption: Classification, incidence and histogenesis of testicular tumours.
summary: The image presents a flowchart detailing the classification, incidence, and histogenesis of testicular tumours. It categorizes these tumours into three main groups: Sex Cord-Stromal Tumours (5%), Germ Cell Tumours (95%), and Combined Tumours (Rare). The Germ Cell Tumours are further subdivided based on differentiation into Spermatocytic (45% Classic Seminoma, 5% Spermatocytic Seminoma) and Non-spermatocytic types (40% Embryonal Carcinoma, 5% Yolk Sac Tumour, Rare Choriocarcinoma, 5% Teratoma).

caption: Seminoma testis. The testis is enlarged but without distorting its contour. Sectioned surface shows replacement of the entire testis by lobulated, homogeneous, grey-white mass.
summary: The image displays a testis affected by seminoma, characterized by an enlarged but contour-preserving organ with a sectioned surface showing replacement by a homogeneous, lobulated, grey-white mass.

caption: Seminoma testis. Microscopy of the tumour shows lobules of monomorphic seminoma cells separated by delicate fibrous stroma containing lymphocytic infiltration.
summary: The image displays a comparison of a schematic diagram and a high-power H&E stained histological section of a Seminoma of the testis. The diagram illustrates the characteristic lobular pattern of the tumor, composed of monomorphic seminoma cells separated by fibrous septa containing lymphocytic infiltrate. The corresponding microscopy image confirms these features, showing sheets of large, uniform tumor cells with distinct cell borders and prominent nucleoli, separated by fibrous bands with scattered lymphocytes.

caption: Yolk sac tumour testis. The tumour has microcystic pattern and has highly anaplastic tumour cells. Several characteristic Schiller- Duval bodies are present. Inset shows intra- and extracellular hyaline globules.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a yolk sac tumor of the testis, characterized by a microcystic pattern and highly anaplastic tumor cells. Key diagnostic features include Schiller-Duval bodies and intra- and extracellular hyaline globules, as highlighted in the inset at higher magnification.

caption: Immature teratoma testis. The testis is enlarged and nodular distorting the testicular contour. Sectioned surface shows replacement of the entire testis by variegated mass having grey-white solid areas, cystic areas, honey-combed areas and foci of cartilage and bone.
summary: The image displays an immature teratoma of the testis, characterized by an enlarged, nodular mass that distorts the normal testicular contour. The sectioned surface reveals a variegated appearance with grey-white solid areas, cystic spaces, honey-combed regions, and foci of cartilage and bone, indicating the presence of multiple tissue types typical of teratomas.

caption: Immature teratoma testis. Microscopy shows a variety of incompletely differentiated tissue elements.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of an immature teratoma of the testis, featuring a schematic diagram on the left and a corresponding H&E stained microscopic section on the right. The diagram illustrates key tissue components including stratified squamous lining, immature stroma, glands, immature cartilage, and neural tissue, while the microscopy image reveals these incompletely differentiated elements at 100x magnification.

caption: Squamous cell carcinoma penis. Microscopy shows whorls of malignant squamous cells with central keratin pearls.
summary: The image displays a histological section of squamous cell carcinoma of the penis, characterized by malignant squamous cells arranged in whorls with central keratin pearls, as seen under H&E staining at 200x magnification.

caption: Normal prostate, benign nodular hyperplasia and prostatic carcinoma. The nodule in case of benign nodular hyperplasia (B) is located in the inner periurethral part and compresses the prostatic urethra while prostatic carcinoma (C) generally arises in the peripheral glands and, thus, does not compress the urethra.
summary: The image illustrates three distinct states of the prostate gland: a normal prostate with an unobstructed urethra, benign nodular hyperplasia characterized by inner periurethral growth compressing the urethra, and prostatic carcinoma arising in the peripheral glands without urethral compression.

caption: Nodular enlargement of the prostate. Sectioned surface of enlarged prostate shows soft to firm, grey-white, nodularity with microcystic areas.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a prostate gland exhibiting significant nodular enlargement. The sectioned surface reveals a soft to firm, grey-white tissue with a distinct nodular architecture and the presence of microcystic areas.

caption: Nodular hyperplasia of the prostate. There is hyperplasia of fibromuscular elements. There are areas of intra-acinar papillary infoldings (convolutions) lined by two layers of epithelium with basal polarity of nuclei.
summary: The image displays histological features of nodular hyperplasia of the prostate, characterized by fibromuscular stromal proliferation and papillary infoldings lined by a double-layered epithelium with basal polarity. Corpora amylacea are also present as eosinophilic secretory concretions within glandular lumens.

caption: Carcinoma of the prostate. The field shows microacini of small malignant cells infiltrating the prostatic stroma. Inset in the photomicrograph shows perineural invasion by prostatic adenocarcinoma.
summary: The image displays histological features of prostatic adenocarcinoma, characterized by back-to-back microacini with loss of convolutions and fibromuscular sling, along with perineural invasion as seen in the inset at higher magnification.

caption: Non-neoplastic epithelial disorders of vulval skin.
summary: The image presents a comparative histological analysis of two non-neoplastic epithelial disorders affecting the vulval skin: Lichen Sclerosus (Panel A) and Squamous Hyperplasia (Panel B). The diagrams illustrate the distinct architectural and cellular changes characteristic of each condition, including alterations in the epidermis and dermis.

caption: Vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN) lesion (Bowen’s disease). There is hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, acanthosis, koilocytosis and presence of atypical anaplastic cells throughout the entire thickness of the epithelium. Photomicrograph on right under higher magnifi cation shows mitotic fi gures in the layers of squamous epithelium.
summary: The image displays histological sections of vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN), specifically Bowen's disease, characterized by hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis, acanthosis, koilocytosis, and atypical anaplastic cells throughout the epithelial thickness. The right panel at higher magnification reveals mitotic figures within the squamous epithelium.

caption: Endocervical polyp. The surface is covered by endocervical mucosa with squamous metaplasia. The stromal core is composed of dense fi brous tissue which shows nonspecifi c infl ammation.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an endocervical polyp stained with H&E at 100x magnification. The surface is lined by columnar mucosa exhibiting squamous metaplasia. The underlying stromal core consists of dense fibrous tissue containing nonspecific inflammation, including inflammatory cells and areas of congestion.

caption: Role of human papillomavirus (HPV) in the patho genesis of cervical neoplasia.
summary: The image is a flowchart illustrating the pathogenesis of cervical neoplasia, specifically focusing on the role of Human Papillomavirus (HPV). It outlines the progression from major risk factors (early age at coitus, multiple sexual partners, high-risk male sex partner) to HPV infection. The chart distinguishes between low-risk HPV types (6, 11) leading to episomal infection and condyloma acuminatum (which can regress), and high-risk HPV types (16, 18, 31, 33, 52, 58) leading to viral integration and the development of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN). It details the progression from CIN-1/L-SIL to CIN-2,3/H-SIL, and finally to invasive cervical cancer, driven by the inactivation of suppressor genes (p53, Rb1), activation of cyclin-E, and cofactors like immunodeficiency and smoking.

caption: Squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL). A, L-SIL. The smear shows koilocytes having abundant vacuolated cytoplasm and nuclear enlargement (arrow). B, H-SIL. The squamous cells have scanty cytoplasm and markedly hyperchromatic nuclei having irregular nuclear outlines. The background shows numerous PMNs.
summary: The image displays two types of squamous intraepithelial lesions (SIL) on Pap smears: low-grade SIL (L-SIL) characterized by koilocytes with abundant vacuolated cytoplasm and nuclear enlargement, and high-grade SIL (H-SIL) featuring squamous cells with scanty cytoplasm and markedly hyperchromatic nuclei with irregular outlines, accompanied by numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs) in the background.

caption: Invasive cancer cervix. Common histologic type is epidermoid (squamous cell) carcinoma showing the pattern of a moderately- diff erentiated non-keratinising large cell carcinoma.
summary: The image displays a histological section of invasive cancer of the cervix, specifically a moderately-differentiated non-keratinising large cell carcinoma (epidermoid/squamous cell type). The left panel highlights key features: non-keratinising tumour cells, mitotic figures, and inflammatory cells. The right panel shows the H&E stained tissue at 200x magnification, revealing nests of malignant cells infiltrating stroma.

caption: Sequential physiologic patterns of endometrium.
summary: The image displays four histological sections of the endometrium, each representing a distinct phase of the menstrual cycle: proliferative, early secretory, late secretory, and shedding. The sections are stained with H&E at 200x magnification, illustrating the morphological changes in the endometrial tissue throughout the cycle.

caption: Tuberculous endometritis. The stroma has caseating epithelioid cell granulomas having Langhans’ giant cells and peripheral layer of lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of endometrial tissue stained with H&E, revealing features consistent with tuberculous endometritis. Key pathological findings include caseating granulomas composed of epithelioid cells and Langhans' giant cells, surrounded by a peripheral layer of lymphocytes. The endometrial mucosa is also visible in the section.

caption: Endometriosis in abdominal scar following caesarean section in the past. Dense fi brocollagenic tissue contains endometrial glands, stroma and evidence of preceding old haemorrhage.
summary: The image displays a histological section of abdominal scar tissue, specifically from a site of a previous caesarean section, demonstrating endometriosis. The tissue is characterized by dense fibrocollagenic stroma containing endometrial glands and stroma, along with evidence of old haemorrhage.

caption: Adenomyosis. The endometrial glands are present deep inside the myometrium (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of the uterus stained with H&E at 40x magnification, illustrating the condition of adenomyosis. The key pathological feature shown is the presence of endometrial glands located deep within the myometrium, which is indicated by a black arrow.

caption: Endometrial carcinoma. The most common histologic pattern is well-diff erentiated adenocarcinoma showing closely packed (back- to-back) glands with cytologic atypia.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of endometrial carcinoma, illustrating the most common pattern: well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. The left panel is a schematic diagram highlighting key diagnostic features including back-to-back glands, infiltration into stroma, and cellular atypia. The right panel shows the corresponding high-power H&E stained tissue section (X200 magnification) demonstrating these malignant characteristics.

caption: Leiomyomas. A, Diagrammatic appearance of common locations and characteristic whorled appearance on cut section. B, Sectioned surface of the uterus shows multiple circumscribed, fi rm nodular masses of variable sizes—submucosal (white arrows) and intramural (black arrows) in location having characteristic whorling. C, The opened up uterine cavity shows an intrauterine gestation sac with placenta (white arrow) and a single circumscribed, enlarged, fi rm nodular mass in intramural location (black arrow) having grey-white whorled pattern.
summary: The image illustrates leiomyomas (uterine fibroids) through three panels: A diagram showing their common locations (intramural, subserosal, submucosal) and characteristic whorled appearance; B a gross specimen of the uterus with multiple circumscribed nodular masses in submucosal and intramural locations; and C an opened uterine cavity revealing an intrauterine gestation sac with placenta alongside an intramural leiomyoma.

caption: Leiomyoma uterus. Microscopy shows whorls of smooth muscle cells which are spindle-shaped, having abundant cytoplasm and oval nuclei.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a leiomyoma of the uterus, characterized by a whorled pattern of benign smooth muscle cells with spindle shapes, abundant cytoplasm, and oval nuclei. The left panel highlights specific features such as benign fibroblasts, a whorled pattern, and hyaline change, while the right panel shows a higher magnification (H&E, X200) of the tissue architecture.

caption: Ectopic tubal gestation. The lumen of the fallopian tube is dilated in the middle and contains dark tan, haemorrhagic and friable gestational tissue (sac, foetus and placenta).
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of an ectopic tubal gestation. The fallopian tube is shown with a dilated lumen containing dark tan, hemorrhagic, and friable gestational tissue, which includes a sac, fetus, and placenta.

caption: Structure of the ovary to illustrate origin of ovarian tumours.
summary: The image is a schematic diagram illustrating the normal anatomical structure of the ovary and correlating it with the origins of various ovarian tumors. It details the layers of the ovary (coelomic epithelium, cortex, medulla) and specific structures like the corpus luteum and Graafian follicle, while also categorizing tumors by their cell of origin (surface epithelium, germ cells, sex cord-stromal, and metastatic).

caption: Papillary serous cystadenocarcinoma of the ovary. Microscopic features include stratifi cation of low columnar epithelium lining the inner surface of the cyst and a few psammoma bodies. The stroma shows invasion by clusters of anaplastic tumour cells.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of papillary serous cystadenocarcinoma of the ovary. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key microscopic features: low columnar epithelial lining, stratification, psammoma bodies, and stromal invasion by anaplastic cells. The right panel is a corresponding high-magnification (H&E, X200) photomicrograph showing the actual tissue architecture with these features, including papillary structures lined by stratified epithelium, calcified psammoma bodies, and infiltrative clusters of malignant cells within the stroma.

caption: Mucinous cystadenoma of the ovary. The cyst wall and the septa are lined by a single layer of tall columnar mucin-secreting epithelium with basally-placed nuclei and large apical mucinous vacuoles.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a mucinous cystadenoma of the ovary, characterized by a cyst wall and septa lined with tall columnar mucin-secreting epithelium. The epithelium features basally placed nuclei and prominent apical mucinous vacuoles, as highlighted in both the schematic diagram and the H&E stained microscopic image.

caption: Histogenetic classifi cation of germ cell tumours of the ovary.
summary: The image displays a flowchart illustrating the histogenetic classification of ovarian germ cell tumours. It categorizes these tumours based on the differentiation status of the germ cells into four main groups: No differentiation, Primitive cells, Intraembryonic differentiation, and Extraembryonic differentiation. Each group leads to specific tumour types, such as Dysgerminoma, Embryonal Carcinoma, Teratomas (Mature Cystic, Monodermal, Immature, Malignant), Yolk Sac Tumour, and Choriocarcinoma.

caption: Benign cystic teratoma. Microscopy shows characteristic lining of the cyst wall by epidermis and its appendages. Islands of mature cartilage are also seen.
summary: The image displays a benign cystic teratoma, characterized by a cyst wall lined with stratified squamous epithelium and its appendages (glands), alongside islands of mature cartilage. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these components, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained histological section at 100x magnification.

caption: Dysgerminoma. The histologic appearance is identical to that of seminoma of the testis. Masses of large uniform tumour cells are separated by scanty fi brous stroma that is infi ltrated by lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Dysgerminoma, a type of ovarian germ cell tumor. The H&E stained slide at 200x magnification reveals a lobular pattern composed of large, uniform tumor cells separated by fibrovascular septa containing lymphocytes.

caption: Solid ovarian tumour. Specimen of the uterus, cervix and adnexa shows enlarged ovarian mass (arrow) on one side which on cut section is solid, gray-white and fi rm.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of the uterus, cervix, and adnexa. A large, solid, gray-white, and firm ovarian mass is present on one side, indicated by an arrow, consistent with a solid ovarian tumor.

caption: Endodermal sinus (yolk sac) tumour ovary. The tumour has microcystic pattern and has highly anaplastic tumour cells. Several characteristic Schiller-Duval bodies are present. Inset shows intra- and extracellular hyaline globules.
summary: This H&E stained histological image (X200 magnification) depicts an endodermal sinus (yolk sac) tumor of the ovary. The tumor is characterized by a microcystic pattern and highly anaplastic tumor cells. Key diagnostic features include the presence of Schiller-Duval bodies and intra- and extracellular hyaline globules, which are highlighted in the inset.

caption: Granulosa cell tumour showing uniform granulosa cells and numerous rosette-like Call-Exner bodies containing central amorphous pink material surrounded by granulosa cells.
summary: The image displays a granulosa cell tumor characterized by uniform granulosa cells arranged in a microfollicular pattern, with numerous rosette-like Call-Exner bodies containing central amorphous pink material surrounded by granulosa cells.

caption: Krukenberg tumour. Histologic features include mucin-fi lled signet-ring cells and richly cellular proliferation of the ovarian stroma.
summary: The image displays the histologic features of a Krukenberg tumor, characterized by the presence of mucin-filled signet-ring cells and a richly cellular proliferation of the ovarian stroma, which exhibits a sarcoma-like appearance.
