caption: Krukenberg tumour showing characteristic bilateral metastatic ovarian cancer (arrow) having fi rm white appearance.
summary: The image displays a schematic diagram and a gross pathology specimen illustrating a bilateral Krukenberg tumor, which is a metastatic ovarian cancer characterized by firm, white nodules in both ovaries.

caption: Uterus containing hydatidiform mole. The specimen shows numerous, variable-sized, grape-like translucent vesicles containing clear fl uid. Tan areas of haemorrhage are also seen.
summary: The image displays a hydatidiform mole, a type of gestational trophoblastic disease, characterized by the presence of numerous grape-like vesicles within the uterus. The left panel illustrates the gross appearance with labeled grape-like vesicles, while the right panel shows a gross specimen with translucent vesicles and areas of hemorrhage.

caption: Hydatidiform mole characterised by hydropic and avascular enlarged villi with trophoblastic proliferation in the form of masses and sheets.
summary: The image displays histological sections of a hydatidiform mole, characterized by hydropic and avascular enlarged villi with trophoblastic proliferation forming masses and sheets. The left panel shows a schematic diagram highlighting the hydropic villous core, syncytiotrophoblast, cytotrophoblast, and trophoblastic proliferation, while the right panel presents an H&E stained section at 200x magnification illustrating the corresponding pathological features.

caption: A, Microanatomy of the breast and major lesions at various sites. B, Normal terminal duct-lobular unit (TDLU). C, SMA-100 immunostain for myoepithelium.
summary: The image illustrates the microanatomy of the breast, highlighting key structures such as ductules, terminal ducts, collecting ducts, adipose tissue, lobes, lobules, lactiferous ducts, lactiferous sinuses, and the nipple. It also depicts various breast lesions including Paget's disease, adenoma of the nipple, intraductal papilloma, fat necrosis, hyperplasias, carcinomas, fibroadenomas, and cysts. Panel B shows a normal terminal duct-lobular unit (TDLU) stained with H&E at 200x magnification, while Panel C demonstrates SMA-IHC staining for myoepithelial cells at 200x magnification.

caption: Simple fibrocystic change. A, Diagrammatic view. It shows cystic dilatation of ducts and increase in fibrous stroma. There is mild epithelial hyperplasia in terminal ducts. B, Non-proliferative fibrocystic changes —fibrosis, cyst formation, adenosis and apocrine metaplasia. C, Proliferative fibrocystic changes showing moderate epithelial hyperplasia.
summary: The image illustrates simple fibrocystic changes of the breast, depicted through a diagrammatic view (A) and histological sections (B and C). The diagram highlights key features including dilated ducts, increased fibrous stroma, adenosis, and apocrine metaplasia. Histological sections show non-proliferative changes with fibrosis, cyst formation, adenosis, and apocrine metaplasia, as well as proliferative changes characterized by moderate epithelial hyperplasia.

caption: Fibroadenoma of the breast, microscopic patterns.
summary: The image displays two distinct microscopic patterns of fibroadenoma of the breast: (A) Intracanalicular pattern, characterized by compressed ducts within a collageneic stroma; and (B) Pericanalicular pattern, where patent ducts are surrounded by a collageneic stroma. Both sections are stained with H&E at X100 magnification.

caption: Phyllodes tumour. Simple mastectomy specimen shows replacement of almost whole breast with a large circumscribed, grey- white, firm, nodular mass having slit-like, compressed cystic areas (arrow) and areas of haemorrhage.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a mastectomy showing a large, circumscribed, grey-white, firm, nodular mass that replaces most of the breast tissue. The mass features slit-like, compressed cystic areas (indicated by an arrow) and regions of hemorrhage, consistent with the diagnosis of a phyllodes tumor.

caption: Topographic considerations in breast cancer.
summary: The image illustrates the topographic distribution of breast cancer, highlighting that the left breast is more commonly affected than the right. It also shows the percentage distribution of tumors across different quadrants of the breast, with the upper outer quadrant being the most common site.

caption: Morphologic patterns in non-invasive (in situ) carcinoma of breast.
summary: The image illustrates the morphologic patterns of non-invasive (in situ) carcinoma of the breast, specifically highlighting ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) with various patterns (solid, comedo, papillary, cribriform) and lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) characterized by uniform cells.

caption: Infiltrating duct carcinoma-NOS. The breast shows a tumour extending up to nipple and areola. Cut surface shows a grey white firm tumour extending irregularly into adjacent breast parenchyma.
summary: The image pair illustrates Infiltrating Duct Carcinoma - Not Otherwise Specified (NOS) of the breast. The diagram depicts a diffusely infiltrating tumor extending into the breast parenchyma and reaching the nipple-areolar complex. The gross pathology specimen on the right shows a firm, grey-white tumor with irregular borders infiltrating the surrounding breast tissue.

caption: Infiltrating duct carcinoma-NOS. Microscopic features include formation of solid nests, cords, gland-like structures and intraductal growth pattern of anaplastic tumour cells. There is infiltration of densely collagenised stroma by these cells in a haphazard manner.
summary: The image displays a histological comparison of Infiltrating Duct Carcinoma - NOS (Not Otherwise Specified). The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key pathological features: stromal invasion, intraductal anaplastic cells, and comedo necrosis. The right panel is a corresponding H&E stained micrograph (X200 magnification) showing solid nests and cords of malignant cells infiltrating a densely collagenized stroma, consistent with the described intraductal growth pattern.

caption: Invasive lobular carcinoma. Characteristic histologic features are: one cell wide files of round regular tumour cells (‘Indian file’ arrangement) infiltrating the stroma and arranged circumferentially around ducts in a target-like pattern.
summary: The image displays the characteristic histologic features of invasive lobular carcinoma, including uniform tumor cells arranged in a single-file pattern ('Indian file') infiltrating the stroma and forming target-like structures around ducts.

caption: Medullary carcinoma breast. Cut surface of the breast shows a large grey white soft fleshy tumour replacing almost whole of the breast. The tumour is somewhat delineated from the adjacent breast parenchyma (arrow) as compared to irregular margin of IDC.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a breast with medullary carcinoma. The cut surface reveals a large, grey-white, soft, fleshy tumor that replaces nearly the entire breast tissue. An arrow points to the interface where the tumor is somewhat delineated from the adjacent breast parenchyma, contrasting with the typically irregular margins seen in invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC).

caption: Medullary carcinoma breast. Microscopy shows two characteristic features—large tumour cells forming syncytial arrange- ment and stroma infiltrated richly with lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of medullary carcinoma of the breast, stained with H&E at 200x magnification. Key features include large anaplastic tumor cells arranged in a syncytial pattern and a stroma richly infiltrated with lymphocytes.

caption: Colloid (mucinous) carcinoma breast. The tumour cells are seen as clusters floating in pools of abundant mucin.
summary: The image displays a histological section of colloid (mucinous) carcinoma of the breast, characterized by clusters of anaplastic tumor cells floating in pools of abundant extracellular mucin. The tissue is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 100x magnification.

caption: Oestrogen and progesterone hormonal receptors (ER and PR) in breast cancer. The tumour cells show nuclear positivity with ER and PR antibody immunostains.
summary: The image displays immunohistochemical staining for oestrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) in breast cancer tissue. Both panels show nuclear positivity, indicating the presence of hormone receptors within the tumour cells, which is crucial for determining treatment options such as hormonal therapy.

caption: Main structures identified in a section of the normal skin.
summary: The image is a schematic diagram illustrating the main anatomical structures of normal skin, organized into three primary layers: the epidermis, dermis (further divided into papillary and reticular layers), and subcutis. It details the location and relationships of key appendages including hair follicles, sebaceous glands, arrector pili muscles, and sweat glands (both eccrine and apocrine).

caption: Different layers comprising the normal epidermis.
summary: The image illustrates the histological structure of the normal epidermis, showcasing its stratified squamous epithelium composed of five distinct layers: stratum corneum, stratum lucidum, stratum granulosum, stratum spinosum (also known as stratum malpighii), and stratum germinativum. Key cell types such as keratinocytes and melanocytes are labeled, with keratinocytes forming the bulk of the layers and melanocytes residing in the stratum germinativum.

caption: Chronic dermatitis (eczema). The epidermis shows hyper keratosis, acanthosis and broadened papillae and spongiosis of the epidermal layers. The dermis shows mild perivascular chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin exhibiting features of chronic dermatitis (eczema). Key findings include epidermal hyperkeratosis, acanthosis, broadened dermal papillae, and spongiosis. The underlying dermis demonstrates a mild perivascular chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate.

caption: Typical appearance of a verruca. The histologic features include papillomatosis, acanthosis, hyperkeratosis with parakeratosis and elongated rete ridges appearing to point towards the centre. Foci of vacuolated cells (koilocytes) are found in the upper stratum malpighii. Inset shows koilocytes and virus-infected keratinocytes containing prominent keratohyaline granules.
summary: The image displays the histological features of a verruca, characterized by papillomatosis, acanthosis, hyperkeratosis with parakeratosis, and elongated rete ridges. Vacuolated cells (koilocytes) are present in the upper stratum malpighii, indicating viral infection. An inset highlights koilocytes and virus-infected keratinocytes with prominent keratohyaline granules.

caption: Molluscum contagiosum. The epidermal layers show numerous molluscum bodies which are intracytoplasmic inclusions. Inset shows close-up view of molluscum bodies.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin affected by Molluscum contagiosum, characterized by the presence of numerous molluscum bodies within the epidermal layers. An inset provides a magnified view of these intracytoplasmic inclusions.

caption: Tinea capitis in the skin. The stratum corneum around the hair follicle shows presence of numerous arthrospores and hyphae.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a skin biopsy stained with PAS at 600x magnification, demonstrating a fungal infection. The stratum corneum surrounding a hair follicle is heavily infiltrated with numerous purple-stained arthroconidia (arthrospores) and hyphae, consistent with the diagnosis of Tinea capitis.

caption: Lupus vulgaris. The dermis contains caseating epithelioid cell granulomas having giant cells and lymphocytes.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, demonstrating the pathology of Lupus vulgaris. The dermis exhibits a dense inflammatory infiltrate characterized by caseating epithelioid cell granulomas, which contain multinucleated giant cells and lymphocytes.

caption: Cutaneous sarcoidosis. The dermis shows non-caseating epithelioid granulomas having Langhans’ giant cells and paucity of lymphocytes, termed as naked granulomas.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin affected by cutaneous sarcoidosis. The dermis exhibits non-caseating epithelioid granulomas characterized by the presence of Langhans' giant cells and a notable paucity of lymphocytes, a feature described as 'naked granulomas'.

caption: Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE). The epidermis is flat and is devoid of rete ridges. There is hyperkeratosis and follicular plugging. The basal layer shows hydropic degeneration and loss of dermo-epidermal junction (arrow). The upper dermis is oedematous and shows moderate lymphocytic infiltration.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin affected by discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE), characterized by a flat epidermis lacking rete ridges, hyperkeratosis, follicular plugging, hydropic degeneration of the basal layer, loss of the dermo-epidermal junction, and moderate lymphocytic infiltration in the upper dermis.

caption: Location of bullae and vesicles in non-infectious bullous dermatoses. A, Pemphigus vulgaris: The bulla is predominantly suprabasilar in position and contains acantholytic cells. B, Pemphigus vegetans: An intraepidermal abscess composed of eosinophils is seen. C, Pemphigus foliaceous: The bulla is superficial in subcorneal position and contains acantholytic cells. D, Pemphigoid: The bulla containing eosinophilis is subepidermal with regeneration of the epidermis at the periphery. E, Dermatitis herpetiformis: There is a papillary microabscess composed of neutrophils. F, Erythema Multiforme: The affected area shows necrotic keratinocytes and inflammatory cells.
summary: The image illustrates the histopathological features of six non-infectious bullous dermatoses, highlighting the location of bullae/vesicles and associated cellular changes within the epidermis and dermis.

caption: Pemphigus vulgaris. An intraepidermal bulla in suprabasal location containing acantholytic cells (arrow) is seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin stained with H&E at 200x magnification, showing an intraepidermal bulla in a suprabasal location. The bulla contains acantholytic cells, which are indicative of pemphigus vulgaris, an autoimmune blistering disease.

caption: Bullous pemphigoid. The skin shows non-acantholytic subepidermal bulla containing microabscess of eosinophils (arrow).
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, demonstrating the features of Bullous Pemphigoid. The key finding is a subepidermal bulla (blister) that is non-acantholytic, containing a microabscess composed of eosinophils, indicated by a black arrow.

caption: Dermatitis herpetiformis. The tips of dermal papillae show neutrophilic microabscess (arrow) causing dermo-epidermal separation at tips.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, illustrating the characteristic features of Dermatitis Herpetiformis. The pathology is defined by neutrophilic microabscesses located at the tips of the dermal papillae, which leads to the separation of the dermis from the epidermis at these sites.

caption: Erythema multiforme. There is pronounced dermo- epidermal interface dermatitis. There is oedema and necrosis of kertinocytes at the junction and mild lymphocytic infiltrate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, demonstrating features consistent with Erythema Multiforme. Key pathological findings include pronounced dermo-epidermal interface dermatitis, characterized by edema and necrosis of keratinocytes at the junction, accompanied by a mild lymphocytic infiltrate.

caption: Psoriasis. There is regular elongation of the rete ridges with thickening of their lower portion. The papillae are elongated and oedematous with suprapapillary thinning of epidermis. There is marked parakeratosis with diagnostic Munro microabscesses in the parakeratotic layer.
summary: The image displays histopathological features of psoriasis, including regular elongation of rete ridges, thickened rete ridges, broadened papillae, suprapapillary thinning of the epidermis, marked parakeratosis, and Munro microabscesses in the parakeratotic layer, as seen in both a schematic diagram and an H&E-stained tissue section at 100x magnification.

caption: Lichen planus. There is hyperkeratosis, focal hypergranulosis and irregular acanthosis with elongated saw-toothed rete ridges. The basal layer shows liquefactive degeneration. The upper dermis shows a band-like mononuclear infiltrate with a sharply-demarcated lower border.
summary: The image displays a schematic diagram and corresponding H&E-stained histological section illustrating the classic features of lichen planus, including hyperkeratosis, focal hypergranulosis, irregular acanthosis with elongated saw-toothed rete ridges, basal layer liquefactive degeneration, and a band-like mononuclear infiltrate in the upper dermis.

caption: Idiopathic calcinosis cutis. The subcutaneous tissue shows masses or nodules of calcium salt surrounded by foreign body giant cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of skin stained with H&E at 100x magnification, illustrating idiopathic calcinosis cutis. The subcutaneous tissue contains masses or nodules of calcium salts, which are surrounded by foreign body giant cells, indicating a chronic inflammatory response to the calcium deposits.

caption: Seborrheic keratosis. The border of the elevated lesion at the lateral margin is in a straight line from the normal uninvolved epidermis (arrow). The other features include papillomatosis, hyper- keratosis and acanthosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of seborrheic keratosis, characterized by an elevated lesion with a straight lateral border transitioning from normal epidermis. Key features include papillomatosis, hyperkeratosis, and acanthosis.

caption: Fibroepithelial polyp (acrochordon or soft fibroma). The epidermis is raised as polypoid mass over dense hyalinised fibrous connective tissue in the dermis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a fibroepithelial polyp (acrochordon or soft fibroma) stained with H&E at 40x magnification. The epidermis is elevated into a polypoid mass overlying dense, hyalinized fibrous connective tissue in the dermis.

caption: Squamous cell papilloma. A, The skin surface shows a papillary growth with a pedicle while the surface is smooth. B, Microscopy resembles verruca but differs from it by not having vacuolated koilocytic cells in stratum malpighii.
summary: The image displays a squamous cell papilloma, characterized by a pedunculated, smooth-surfaced papillary growth on the skin (Panel A). Histologically (Panel B), it resembles a verruca but lacks the diagnostic feature of vacuolated koilocytic cells in the stratum malpighii, as confirmed by H&E staining at 40x magnification.

caption: Epidermal inclusion cyst. The cyst wall is composed of all layers of the epidermis and has laminated layers of keratin towards the lumen of the cyst.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an epidermal inclusion cyst, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 40x magnification. The cyst wall is composed of stratified squamous epithelium resembling the epidermis, and the lumen contains laminated layers of keratin.

caption: Dermoid cyst. In addition to features of epidermal cyst, dermoid cyst has adnexal structures in the cyst wall (i.e. hair follicles, sweat and sebaceous glands).
summary: The image displays a histological section of a dermoid cyst, characterized by a cyst wall containing epidermal elements and adnexal structures such as hair follicles, sweat glands, and sebaceous glands, distinguishing it from a simple epidermal cyst.

caption: Bowen’s disease. The epidermis is thick with loss of rete ridges but the normal base to surface maturation of epidermal layers is effaced. Instead, there are bizarre atypical squamous cells but the border between the epidermis and dermis is intact i.e. the basement membrane is not breached.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Bowen's disease, characterized by full-thickness epidermal atypia with loss of rete ridges and absence of normal maturation from base to surface. The atypical squamous cells are present throughout the epidermis without breaching the basement membrane, indicating in situ carcinoma.

caption: Microscopic features of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The dermis is invaded by downward proliferating epidermal masses of cells which show atypical features. A few horn pearls with central laminated keratin are present. There is inflammatory reaction in the dermis between the masses of tumour cells.
summary: The image displays microscopic features of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by downward proliferating masses of atypical epidermal cells invading the dermis, with keratin pearls (laminated keratin structures) and associated inflammatory reaction in the dermis.

caption: Common location and macroscopic appearance of basal cell carcinoma (rodent ulcer).
summary: The image illustrates the common location and macroscopic appearance of basal cell carcinoma, also known as a rodent ulcer, depicted as a red, irregular lesion on the face of an elderly individual.

caption: Solid basal cell carcinoma. The dermis is invaded by irregular masses of basaloid cells with characteristic peripheral palisaded appearance. The masses of tumour cells are separated from dermal collagen by a space called shrinkage artefact.
summary: The image displays a solid basal cell carcinoma, characterized by irregular masses of basaloid tumor cells invading the dermis. These cells exhibit a characteristic peripheral palisaded arrangement. The tumor masses are separated from the surrounding dermal collagen by a cleft-like space known as a shrinkage artefact. The image includes a schematic diagram illustrating these features alongside a corresponding H&E stained histological section.

caption: Pilomatricoma (calcifying epithelioma of Malherbe). The tumour shows islands and lobules within the dermis which are composed of two types of cells: outer basophilic cells and inner shadow cells. Areas of calcification are also seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a pilomatricoma (calcifying epithelioma of Malherbe), characterized by islands and lobules within the dermis composed of basophilic cells and shadow cells, with areas of calcification present.

caption: Intradermal naevus showing nests of naevus cells which are typically uniform and present in the dermis. Melanin pigment in naevus cells is coarse and irregular.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an intradermal naevus stained with H&E at 200x magnification. The dermis contains nests of naevus cells that are described as uniform. Coarse and irregular melanin pigment is visible within these naevus cells.

caption: Malignant melanoma of the oral cavity. The hemi- maxillectomy specimen shows an elevated blackish ulcerated area with irregular outlines.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a hemi-maxillectomy showing malignant melanoma of the oral cavity. The specimen features an elevated, blackish, ulcerated area with irregular outlines, characteristic of melanoma invasion into the maxillary bone and soft tissues.

caption: Malignant melanoma shows junctional activity at the dermal-epidermal junction. Tumour cells resembling epithelioid cells with pleomorphic nuclei and prominent nucleoli are seen as solid masses in the dermis. Many of the tumour cells contain fine granular melanin pigment. Photomicrograph shows a prominent atypical mitotic figure (arrow).
summary: The image pair illustrates malignant melanoma, characterized by junctional activity at the dermal-epidermal junction, with tumor cells resembling epithelioid cells exhibiting pleomorphic nuclei and prominent nucleoli. Fine granular melanin pigment is present within many tumor cells, and a prominent atypical mitotic figure is highlighted in the photomicrograph.

caption: Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans. The tumour cells are arranged in storiform or cartwheel pattern. The tumour cells are spindled admixed with histiocytes and show moderate anisocytosis and anisonucleosis.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP) stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification. The tissue exhibits a characteristic storiform or cartwheel pattern formed by spindled tumor cells. These cells are admixed with histiocytes and demonstrate moderate anisocytosis and anisonucleosis.

caption: Appearance in functional disorders of the thyroid gland.
summary: The image illustrates the characteristic facial appearances associated with three distinct functional disorders of the thyroid gland: hyperthyroidism, cretinism, and myxoedema. Each panel depicts a person with specific physical features corresponding to the condition labeled below.

caption: Pathogenesis of simple and nodular goitre.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of simple and nodular goitre, showing how iodine lack and goitrogens lead to deficient thyroid hormone production, excessive TSH stimulation, cyclic hyperplasia-involution, diffuse goitre, and eventually nodular goitre through fibrosis of involuted areas and growth of hyperplastic areas.

caption: Role of parathormone in regulating calcium metabo lism in the body.
summary: The image illustrates the regulatory role of parathyroid hormone (PTH) in calcium metabolism. It depicts a feedback loop where decreased serum calcium stimulates PTH secretion from the parathyroid gland, which then acts on the bones, kidneys, and gastrointestinal tract to increase serum calcium levels, subsequently reducing PTH secretion.

caption: Major clinical manifestations of hyperparathyroidism.
summary: The image illustrates the major clinical manifestations of hyperparathyroidism, a condition characterized by excessive parathyroid hormone (PTH) secretion. It depicts systemic effects including emotional disturbances, hypercalcemia, muscle atrophy, osteitis fibrosa cystica, peptic ulcer, pancreatitis, renal calculi, and nephrocalcinosis, highlighting the widespread impact of this endocrine disorder on multiple organ systems.

caption: A, Pathway of normal insulin synthesis and release in β-cells of pancreatic islets. B, Chain of events in action of insulin on target cell.
summary: The image illustrates two key biological processes: (A) the synthesis and release of insulin in β-cells of pancreatic islets, triggered by hyperglycemia, and (B) the downstream actions of insulin on target cells, including anabolic and mitogenic effects mediated through the insulin receptor and IRS proteins.
entities: A and B chains with C-peptide

caption: Amyloidosis of the pancreatic islet tissue. The islets are mostly replaced by structureless eosinophilic material which stains positively with Congo red.
summary: The image displays pancreatic islet tissue affected by amyloidosis. On the left, an H&E stain at 400x magnification reveals islets largely replaced by amorphous, eosinophilic material. On the right, a Congo red stain at 200x magnification demonstrates that this material exhibits apple-green birefringence under polarized light (though not explicitly shown here, the positive Congo red reaction is indicated), confirming the presence of amyloid deposits within the islets.

caption: Pathophysiological basis of common signs and symptoms due to uncontrolled hyperglycaemia in diabetes mellitus.
summary: The image illustrates the pathophysiological cascade initiated by insulin deficiency in diabetes mellitus, leading to hyperglycaemia and its downstream effects. It details how hyperglycaemia causes glycosuria, which triggers osmotic diuresis (resulting in polyuria, polydipsia, tachycardia, hypotension) and decreased anabolism. Simultaneously, increased catabolism leads to hunger, polyphagia, weight loss, and wasting. The accumulation of free fatty acids and their oxidation in the liver results in ketoacidosis. Both ketoacidosis and diabetic coma are terminal states that can lead to death.

caption: Long-term complications of diabetes mellitus. A, Pathogenesis. B, Secondary systemic complications.
summary: The image illustrates the long-term complications of diabetes mellitus, categorized into vascular, metabolic, and immune mechanisms. Vascular complications include macrovascular issues like atherosclerosis and microvascular issues such as neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy. Metabolic complications involve sorbitol accumulation and protein glycosylation leading to lesions in the aorta, lens of the eye, kidney, and nerves, as well as basement membrane thickening. Immune complications arise from defective chemotaxis and PMN function, resulting in infections like tuberculosis, UTI, pneumonia, and fungal infections. The secondary systemic complications depicted include cataract, retinopathy, coronary atherosclerosis, IHD, autonomic dysfunction, infarct bowel, diabetic nephropathy, UTI, diabetic neuropathy, occlusive atherosclerosis, diabetic ulcer, and dry gangrene.

caption: The glucose tolerance test, showing blood glucose curves (venous blood glucose) and glucosuria after 75 gm of oral glucose.
summary: The image displays four glucose tolerance test curves illustrating different physiological responses to a 75 gm oral glucose load. Each graph plots blood sugar levels (mg/dl) over time (hours) and indicates glucosuria levels. The curves represent: A) Normal response, B) Renal glucosuria, C) Alimentary (lag storage) glucosuria, and D) Moderate diabetes mellitus, with renal threshold lines marked for comparison.

caption: Chronic suppurative osteomyelitis. Histologic appearance shows necrotic bone and extensive purulent inflammatory exudate.
summary: The image displays a histological section of chronic suppurative osteomyelitis, characterized by necrotic bone and extensive purulent inflammatory exudate. The left panel provides a schematic diagram illustrating the key features: necrotic bone (dark blue areas), capillaries (small vessels within the bone trabeculae), and purulent exudate (collections of neutrophils). The right panel shows the corresponding high-magnification H&E stained tissue section, revealing dense aggregates of inflammatory cells (neutrophils) surrounding areas of necrotic bone.

caption: Pathogenesis of pyogenic osteomyelitis. A, The process begins as a focus of microabscess in a vascular loop in the marrow which expands to stimulate resorption of adjacent bony trabeculae. Simultaneously, there is beginning of reactive woven bone formation by the periosteum. B, The abscess expands further causing necrosis of the cortex called sequestrum. The formation of viable new reactive bone surrounding the sequestrum is called involucrum. The extension of infection into the joint space, epiphysis and the skin produces a draining sinus.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of pyogenic osteomyelitis, showing two stages: (A) initial microabscess formation in the marrow space leading to bony resorption and reactive woven bone formation; (B) progression to cortical necrosis (sequestrum), surrounded by new bone (involucrum), with infection extending into the epiphysis, joint space, and skin, forming a draining sinus.

caption: Osteomyelitis of the vertebral body.
summary: The image illustrates two distinct types of vertebral osteomyelitis: bacterial (left) and tuberculous (right). In bacterial osteomyelitis, pus extends from the infected vertebral body into the intervertebral disc, leading to discitis. In tuberculous osteomyelitis, the vertebral body collapses due to caseous necrosis, and pus extends anteriorly, often causing a psoas abscess. The intact vertebrae and discs are shown for comparison.

caption: Tuberculous osteomyelitis. There are epithelioid cell granulomas with minute areas of caseation necrosis and surrounded by Langhans’ giant cells. Pieces of necrotic bone are also seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of tuberculous osteomyelitis, characterized by the presence of epithelioid cell granulomas containing minute areas of caseation necrosis. These granulomas are surrounded by Langhans' giant cells, and fragments of necrotic bone are also visible within the tissue.

caption: Pathogenesis of renal osteodystrophy in chronic renal failure. Circled serial numbers in the graphic representation correspond to the sequence described in the text under pathogenesis.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of renal osteodystrophy in chronic renal failure, detailing the sequence of events leading to various bone disorders. It shows how chronic renal failure leads to decreased conversion of 25(OH) cholecalciferol to 1,25(OH)2 cholecalciferol, reduced intestinal calcium absorption, and increased serum phosphate levels, which collectively result in elevated PTH secretion. This cascade ultimately causes osteomalacia, osteitis fibrosa cystica, metastatic calcification, and secondary hyperparathyroidism. Additionally, the diagram highlights the role of metabolic acidosis in contributing to osteoporosis.

caption: Fibrous dysplasia of the bone. The bony trabeculae have fish­hook appearance (or Chinese­letter appearance) surrounded by fibrous tissue. The osteoblastic rimming of the bony trabeculae are characteristically absent.
summary: The image displays a histological section of fibrous dysplasia of the bone, characterized by irregular, curvilinear bony trabeculae with a 'fish-hook' or 'Chinese-letter' appearance, embedded within a cellular fibrous stroma. Notably, the bony trabeculae lack osteoblastic rimming, which is a key diagnostic feature distinguishing this condition from other bone lesions.

caption: Anatomic locations of common primary bone and cartilage tumours.
summary: The image illustrates the anatomic locations of common primary bone and cartilage tumors, categorized by their benign or malignant nature and their position along the long bone (femur and tibia). Tumors are shown in relation to the diaphysis, metaphysis, and epiphysis of the bone.

caption: Osteochondroma. The overlying cap shows mature cartilage cells covering the underlying mature lamellar bone containing marrow spaces.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an osteochondroma, characterized by a cartilaginous cap covering underlying mature lamellar bone with marrow spaces. The tissue is stained using H&E at 40x magnification.

caption: Chondrosarcoma, scapula. The bone is expanded externally due to a gelatinous tumour. Sectioned surface shows lobulated mass with bluish cartilaginous hue infiltrating the soft tissues.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a chondrosarcoma originating from the scapula. The bone is visibly expanded by a gelatinous, lobulated tumor mass with a characteristic bluish cartilaginous hue. The sectioned surface reveals this infiltrative mass extending into and invading the surrounding soft tissues.

caption: Osteoclastoma. Microscopy reveals osteoclast­like multinucleate giant cells which are regularly distributed among the mononuclear stromal cells.
summary: The image displays a comparative view of Osteoclastoma (Giant Cell Tumor of Bone), featuring a schematic diagram on the left illustrating the characteristic histological pattern of evenly distributed, regularly spaced osteoclastic giant cells within a background of mononuclear stromal cells. The right panel presents a corresponding high-power H&E stained micrograph (X200) demonstrating this classic histological appearance with numerous multinucleated giant cells scattered among the stromal cell population.

caption: Ewing’s sarcoma. The tumour is largely extending into soft tissues including the skeletal muscle. Cut surface of the tumour is grey­white, cystic, soft and friable.
summary: The image illustrates a cross-section of a bone affected by Ewing's sarcoma, highlighting key pathological features such as a diaphyseal tumor, widened shaft, and necrotic and haemorrhagic areas. The caption further describes the tumor's extension into soft tissues, including skeletal muscle, and notes the cut surface characteristics as grey-white, cystic, soft, and friable.

caption: Chordoma. The tumour cells are quite variable in size having characteristic bubbly cytoplasm (physaliphorous cells) and anisonucleocytosis. The background is myxoid.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Chordoma, characterized by a myxoid background and a population of tumour cells with variable sizes. The cells exhibit distinct bubbly cytoplasm, known as physaliphorous cells, and show anisonucleocytosis (variation in nuclear size). The tissue is stained using H&E at 200x magnification.

caption: Osseous deposits from carcinoma breast.
summary: The image displays a histological section stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, revealing metastatic carcinoma of the breast. The key pathological finding is the presence of osseous (bone) tissue, characterized by trabeculae of lamellar bone, which is being infiltrated and replaced by malignant tumor cells, indicating bone metastasis.

caption: Pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis.
summary: The image illustrates the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis, starting with genetic susceptibility (MHC class II) and antigenic stimulation leading to CD4+ T-cell activation. This activation triggers the release of cytokines (TNF-α, INF-γ, IL-1), which activate B-cells, endothelial cells, and macrophages. Activated B-cells produce anti-IgG antibodies (rheumatoid factor), endothelial cells release adhesion molecules, and macrophages release cytokines and proteases. These processes lead to the formation of immune complexes, inflammatory cells, and pannus, causing inflammatory damage to the synovium, small vessels, and collagen. Ultimately, this results in the destruction of cartilage, bone, fibrosis, ankylosis, and joint deformities.

caption: Rheumatoid arthritis. The characteristic histologic features are villous hypertrophy of the synovium and marked mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrate in synovial membrane with formation of lymphoid follicles at places.
summary: The image displays histological features of rheumatoid arthritis, characterized by villous hypertrophy of the synovium, marked mononuclear inflammatory cell infiltrate (lymphocytes and plasma cells), fibrin deposition, and the formation of lymphoid follicles within the synovial membrane.

caption: A gouty tophus, showing central aggregates of urate crystals surrounded by inflammatory cells, fibroblasts and occasional giant cells.
summary: The image displays a gouty tophus, characterized by central aggregates of urate crystals surrounded by inflammatory cells, fibroblasts, and occasional giant cells. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these components, while the right panel shows a histological section stained with H&E at 400x magnification, highlighting the dense purple urate crystal core and surrounding cellular reaction.

caption: Tenosynovial giant cell tumour. The tumour shows infiltrate of small oval to spindled histiocytes with numerous interspersed multinucleate giant cells lying in a background of fibrous tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor (TGCT) stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification. The tissue architecture is characterized by a dense infiltrate of small, oval to spindled histiocytes interspersed with numerous multinucleated giant cells, all set within a background of fibrous connective tissue.

caption: Cyst of ganglion. The cyst wall is composed of dense connective tissue lined internally by flattened lining. The cyst wall shows myxoid degeneration.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a ganglion cyst at 200x magnification using H&E staining. The cyst wall is characterized by dense connective tissue and is lined internally by flattened cells. Notably, the wall exhibits myxoid degeneration.

caption: Fibroma of the oral cavity. The circumscribed lesion is composed of mature collagenised fibrous connective tissue.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a fibroma from the oral cavity, characterized by a circumscribed lesion composed of mature collagenized fibrous connective tissue. The tissue is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 100x magnification.

caption: Fibrosarcoma, common clinical location.
summary: The image displays a clinical illustration of a human lower leg and foot, highlighting a soft tissue sarcoma located on the calf. The tumor is depicted with irregular outlines, indicating its invasive nature, and the caption identifies the specific type of sarcoma as fibrosarcoma.

caption: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma. The lobulated tumour infiltrating the skeletal muscle is somewhat circumscribed. Cut surface is grey-white fleshy with areas of haemorrhage and necrosis.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a malignant fibrous histiocytoma, characterized as a lobulated tumor infiltrating skeletal muscle. The cut surface reveals a grey-white fleshy texture with visible areas of hemorrhage and necrosis.

caption: Fibrosarcoma. Microscopy shows a well-differentiated tumour composed of spindle-shaped cells forming interlacing fascicles producing a typical Herring-bone pattern. A few mitotic figures are also seen.
summary: The image displays a histological section of fibrosarcoma, characterized by spindle-shaped tumor cells arranged in interlacing bundles forming a classic Herring-bone pattern. The diagram highlights key features including pleomorphism, mitotic figures, and the overall architecture of the tumor. The accompanying H&E stained micrograph at 200x magnification confirms the presence of these diagnostic features.

caption: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma (pleomorphic sarcoma). The tumour shows admixture of spindle-shaped pleomorphic cells forming storiform (cart-wheel) pattern and histiocyte-like round to oval cells. Bizarre pleomorphic multinucleate tumour giant cells and some mononuclear inflammatory cells are also present.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Malignant Fibrous Histiocytoma (MFH), a pleomorphic sarcoma. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating key cellular features, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained tissue at 200x magnification. The tumor is characterized by a mixture of spindle-shaped cells arranged in a storiform (cart-wheel) pattern, histiocyte-like round to oval cells, bizarre pleomorphic multinucleate giant cells, and mitotic figures.

caption: Lipoma. The tumour shows a thin capsule and underlying lobules of mature adipose cells separated by delicate fibrous septa.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a lipoma, characterized by a thin fibrous capsule and lobules of mature adipocytes separated by delicate fibrous septa. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating the capsule and mature adipocytes, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E stained tissue section at 100x magnification.

caption: Liposarcoma. The tumour shows characteristic, univacuolated and multi vacuolated lipoblasts with bizarre nuclei. Inset in the right photomicrograph shows close-up view of a typical lipoblast having multivacuolated cytoplasm indenting the atypical nucleus.
summary: The image displays histological features of a liposarcoma, characterized by univacuolated and multivacuolated lipoblasts with bizarre, indented nuclei. The left panel highlights key diagnostic features including pleomorphism, myxoid change, lipoblasts, indented nuclei, and mitotic figures. The right panel shows an H&E-stained section at 200x magnification, with an inset providing a close-up view of a typical multivacuolated lipoblast indenting an atypical nucleus.

caption: Botryoid rhabdomyosarcoma, nose. The tumour shows the characteristic submucosal Cambium layer of tumour cells. The tumour cells are round to oval and have anaplasia.
summary: This H&E stained histological image (X200 magnification) of a nasal tumor demonstrates botryoid rhabdomyosarcoma. The image highlights the characteristic submucosal cambium layer, which is a dense band of tumor cells located beneath the mucosa. The tumor cells are described as round to oval and exhibit anaplasia.

caption: Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma. The tumour is divided into alveolar spaces composed of fibrocollagenous tissue. The fibrous trabeculae are lined by small, dark, undifferentiated tumour cells, with some cells floating in the alveolar spaces. A few multinucleate tumour giant cells are also present.
summary: The image displays a histological section of alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma, characterized by alveolar spaces formed by fibrocollagenous tissue. Fibrous trabeculae are lined by small, dark, undifferentiated tumour cells, with some cells floating in the alveolar spaces. A few multinucleate tumour giant cells are also present.

caption: Classic synovial sarcoma, showing characteristic biphasic cellular pattern. The tumour is composed of epithelial-like cells lining cleft- like spaces and gland-like structures, and spindle cell areas forming fibrosarcoma-like growth pattern.
summary: The image displays a classic synovial sarcoma with a biphasic cellular pattern. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating the two components: epithelial-like cells lining cleft-like spaces and spindle cells forming a fibrosarcoma-like growth pattern, along with features of pleomorphism and mitosis. The right panel is a corresponding H&E stained histological section (X100 magnification) showing the same biphasic architecture with glandular/cleft-like structures and spindle cell areas.

caption: Cells comprising the nervous system.
summary: The image illustrates the cellular components of the nervous system, categorized into three main groups: neurons, neuroglia, and microglia. It provides detailed diagrams of each cell type, highlighting their structural features and functions within the nervous system.

caption: Normal circulation of CSF.
summary: The image illustrates the normal circulation pathway of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) within the brain, showing its flow from the lateral ventricles through the third and fourth ventricles, and finally into the subarachnoid space via specific foramina.

caption: Acute suppurative meningitis.
summary: This H&E stained histological slide at 200x magnification demonstrates acute suppurative meningitis, characterized by the presence of inflammatory exudate within the meninges and the formation of granulation tissue, indicating an active and severe inflammatory response.

caption: Cryptococci in CSF as seen in mucicarmine stain.
summary: The image displays a microscopic view of a cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) sample stained with mucicarmine at 1000x magnification. The stain highlights numerous encapsulated yeast cells, identified as Cryptococci, which appear as distinct magenta or pinkish-purple structures against a pale blue background.

caption: Tuberculous encephalitis of the brain.
summary: The image displays a histological section of brain tissue stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 200x magnification, illustrating the pathological features of tuberculous encephalitis. Key findings include viable brain tissue, an epithelioid cell granuloma, caseation necrosis, and a Langhans' giant cell, which are characteristic of a granulomatous inflammatory response.

caption: Neurocysticercosis. The sliced surface of the cerebral hemisphere of the brain shows may tiny whitish nodules and cysts about 1 cm in diameter.
summary: The image displays a gross pathology specimen of a human brain, specifically showing a coronal section of the cerebral hemispheres. The specimen reveals multiple small, whitish nodules and cysts scattered throughout the brain parenchyma, which are characteristic findings of neurocysticercosis, a parasitic infection caused by the larval stage of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium.

caption: An old cystic infarct in the brain (coronal section). There is shrinkage of scarred area with ipsilateral ventricular dilatation.
summary: The image displays a coronal section of the brain illustrating an old cystic infarct. The scarred area has undergone shrinkage, leading to compensatory dilatation of the ipsilateral ventricle.

caption: An anaemic infarct of a few days duration. The histologic changes are reactive astrocytosis, a few reactive macrophages and neovascularisation in the wall of the cystic lesion. The outer cortical layer is, however, intact.
summary: The image displays a histological section of an anaemic infarct, characterized by reactive gliosis, neovascularisation, a cystic space, and chronic inflammation. The outer cortical layer remains intact, indicating the infarct is in its early stages.

caption: Two types of intracranial haemorrhage: intracerebral (left) and subarachnoid haemorrhage (right).
summary: The image is a schematic diagram illustrating two types of intracranial hemorrhage: intracerebral hemorrhage (left side) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (right side). It depicts the anatomical structures of the skull, dura, subarachnoid space, CSF, sagittal sinus, falx cerebri, and brain tissue. The diagram shows the location and extent of bleeding in each type of hemorrhage, with the intracerebral hemorrhage shown as a localized bleed within the brain parenchyma and the subarachnoid hemorrhage depicted as blood filling the subarachnoid space around the brain.

caption: The circle of Willis showing principal sites of berry (saccular) aneurysms. The serial numbers indicate the frequency of involvement.
summary: The image illustrates the Circle of Willis, a critical arterial network at the base of the brain, highlighting the principal sites where berry (saccular) aneurysms most frequently occur. The diagram labels key arteries and indicates four numbered locations corresponding to the frequency of aneurysm formation.

caption: Epidural haematoma often results from rupture of artery following skull fracture resulting in accumulation of arterial blood between the skull and the dura.
summary: The image illustrates an epidural haematoma, a condition where arterial blood accumulates between the skull and the dura mater following a skull fracture. The diagram shows the dura detached from the skull, the sagittal sinus, falx cerebri, and flattened gyri due to brain oedema caused by the haematoma.

caption: Subdural haematoma often results from rupture of veins crossing the cerebral convexities and is characterised by accumulation of venous blood between the dura and the arachnoid.
summary: The image illustrates a sagittal section of the brain demonstrating an epidural haematoma, which is a collection of venous blood between the dura mater and the skull. The diagram labels key anatomical structures including the dura attached to the skull, sagittal sinus, falx cerebri, arachnoid, and uncompressed gyri, providing a clear view of the spatial relationships and the location of the haematoma.

caption: The anatomic distribution of common tumours.
summary: The image illustrates the anatomic distribution of common brain tumors, highlighting their typical locations within the central nervous system. It includes labeled diagrams of various tumor types such as pinealoma, meningioma, glioma, astrocytoma, craniopharyngioma, pituitary adenoma, ependymoma, and acoustic neuroma, each positioned according to its characteristic site of origin.

caption: Anaplastic astrocytoma, showing hypercellularity of pleomorphic astrocytic cells, mitoses and vascular proliferation in fibrillary background. Areas of necrosis are also present.
summary: The image displays a histological representation of anaplastic astrocytoma, characterized by hypercellularity of pleomorphic astrocytic cells, mitotic figures, vascular proliferation within a fibrillary background, and areas of necrosis. The left panel is a schematic diagram illustrating these features, while the right panel shows the corresponding H&E-stained tissue section at 200x magnification.

caption: Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade IV astrocytoma). The tumour is densely cellular having marked pleomorphism. Characteristically, the tumour has areas of necrosis which are surrounded by a palisade layer of anaplastic tumour cells.
summary: The image displays a histological section of Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade IV astrocytoma), characterized by dense cellularity, marked pleomorphism, geographic necrosis surrounded by a palisade of anaplastic tumor cells, and vascular proliferation.

caption: Ependymoma showing uniform ependymal tumour cells forming rosettes and canaliculi.
summary: The image displays a histological representation of an ependymoma, characterized by uniform ependymal tumor cells arranged in rosettes and canaliculi. The left panel illustrates a schematic diagram highlighting these structures, while the right panel shows a corresponding H&E-stained microscopic view at 200x magnification, demonstrating the cellular architecture typical of this tumor type.

caption: Meningioma, The tumour mass is circumscribed (arrow) with irregular surface convolutions and prominent blood vessels. It is firm in consistency with peripherally adherent thick firm dural tissue. Cut surface of the mass is firm and fibrous.
summary: The image displays a parasagittal meningioma, characterized by a well-circumscribed tumor mass with an irregular, convoluted surface and prominent blood vessels. The tumor is shown both in a schematic diagram and a gross pathology specimen, highlighting its firm consistency and adherence to the dura mater.

caption: Meningioma, transitional type. The cells have features of both syncytial and fibroblastic type and form whorled appearance. Some of the whorls contain psammoma bodies.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a transitional-type meningioma, characterized by a mixture of syncytial and fibroblastic tumor cell patterns. The tissue exhibits a whorled architecture with the presence of psammoma bodies, which are concentric calcified structures within the whorls.

caption: Metastatic tumour deposits in the brain. They are commonly multiple, well-defined and usually located at the grey and white matter junction.
summary: The image displays a diagram of a brain illustrating multiple metastatic tumor deposits. These deposits are depicted as distinct, well-defined masses scattered throughout the brain tissue, specifically located at the junction between the grey and white matter.

caption: Pathologic reaction of peripheral nerve to injury.
summary: The image illustrates the pathologic reactions of peripheral nerves to injury, depicting four distinct stages: A) Normal nerve structure with labeled components including nerve cell body, nucleus, dendrites, axon, node of Ranvier, myelin sheath, Schwann cell, Schwann cell nucleus, and muscle; B) Wallerian degeneration showing disintegration of axon and myelin sheath; C) Axonal degeneration characterized by chromatolysis and accumulated organelles; D) Segmental demyelination featuring Schwann cell proliferation and demyelinated segments.

caption: Schwannoma (neurilemmoma), showing whorls of densely cellular (Antoni A) and loosely cellular (Antoni B) areas with characteristic nuclear palisading (Verocay bodies).
summary: The image displays histological features of a Schwannoma (neurilemmoma), characterized by Antoni A areas (densely cellular) and Antoni B areas (loosely cellular), with nuclear palisading forming Verocay bodies.

caption: Plexiform neurofibromatosis. The main mass is multilobulated with increased fat while lower part of the image shows a separate encapsulated gelatinous mass. Cut surface of both the masses shows circumscribed, gelatinous, lobulated grey-white firm masses.
summary: The image displays gross pathology specimens of plexiform neurofibromatosis. The main mass is multilobulated with increased fat, while a separate encapsulated gelatinous mass is visible in the lower portion. Both masses exhibit a circumscribed, gelatinous, lobulated, grey-white, and firm appearance on the cut surface.

caption: Neurofibroma, showing interlacing bundles of spindle-shaped cells separated by mucoid matrix. The cells have wavy nuclei and a residual nerve fibre (neurite) is also identified.
summary: The image displays a histological section of a neurofibroma, characterized by interlacing bundles of spindle-shaped cells with wavy nuclei embedded in a mucoid (myxoid) matrix. A residual nerve fiber (neurite) is also identified within the tissue architecture.
